The phase fraction achieved  by  targeting  a  1:1 mol  boride/carbide  atomic  ratio  provided important information for optimizing mechanical, thermal, or electrical properties since the carbide to boride ratio can be tailored and used to design dual-phase ceramic compositions.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ = 10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ = 3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma-Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2, ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2, HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described in a previous work. 22 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, previously described, 22 are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density) except monolithic ZrB2 which has a density ∼ 95% as a consequence of O impurities which prevent its full densification; (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

4.2.2. Mechanical behavior
enhanced densification kinetics. ZrB2 -SiC (30 vol %) composites with SiC particle size of 0.45 μ m and 10 μ m resulted in the densification of 99.8% and 97.4%, respectively. The former composite yielded a fracture strength value of 909 MPa while as the later one showed 386 MPa [156]. Thus, it can be concluded that the limitation of lower sinterability in di-borides can be overcome by judicial selection of sintering route, and size and type of reinforcement.
In many applications of UHTCs, especially in the context of their use in a space vehicle, it is mandatory to observe the role of high temperature and surrounding atmosphere during re-entry. Thus, the oxidation resistance becomes a key property to be evaluated by simulating the reentry conditions, which is discussed in section 4.2.3.

5.2. ZrB2 ceramics
Among the carbides, SiC has been extensively used and investigated as a sinter-additive since the SiC addition enhances sinterability, inhibits grain growth and improves mechanical, oxidation and other properties [239-252]. Use of SiC as sinter-additive to ZrB2 has been studied in different ways by varying SiC content (up to ~30 vol%), use of different sizes of SiC starting powder (viz., micron, sub-micron and nano-sized) [239], different forms of SiC (particulate, whisker, fiber) and combining other additives along with SiC. It has been reported that the densification and densification rate of ZrB2 ceramics gets enhanced with increasing SiC-content due to liquid phase formation and control of grain growth [240]. Monteverde reported that with the use of 10 vol% SiC (ultrafine, 0.8 µm), near-theoretical sinter-density could be achieved for ZrB2 after hot press sintering at 1900 °C for 20 min in vacuum [241]. Similarly, in another work, ZrB2 could be fully densified at still lower sintering temperature (HP at 1650 °C, 120 min) by using ~22.4 vol% nano-sized SiC powders [10]. In both cases, liquid phase sintering was reported as main densification mechanism. Liu et al. [242] developed ZrB2-SiC-ZrC system and observed linear relationship between the mechanical properties (Young's modulus and hardness) and the composition. In another work, Khoeini et al. [243] further used carbon as sinter-additive to improve the densification of ZrB2-SiC via pressureless sintering. Removal of ZrO2, B2O3 and SiO2 surface oxides on the starting powder particles aided the densification in that case.

A B S T R A C T
strength and good thermal conductivity, the most studied UHTCs are the ZrB2-based composites [17 -19]. In order to improve the thermo-mechanical properties, oxidation resistance and final density, borides are usually combined with silicon-containing additives in small amount,  such  as  10 -20  vol%  of  SiC  [20,21].  In  fact,  the  oxidation resistance of pure ZrB2  is poor already at 1000 ◦ C, because of the formation of a porous and non-protective ZrO2 film, as well as the evaporation  of  B2O3.  The  introduction of  SiC  promotes  the  formation  of  a surficial glassy silica phase, which protects the material from oxidation up to 1600 ◦ C [22]. Other ceramic additives, such as Y2O3  or MoSi2 improve the densification of ZrB2-SiC composites by forming a liquid phase with the oxide impurities present on the surface of ZrB2 and SiC grains [23,24]. The presence of Y2O3 stabilizes the ZrO2 formed during oxidation in the tetragonal structure, leading to the formation of a more compact film that improves the oxidation resistance [25]. The presence of MoSi2 improves the oxidation resistance thanks to the formation of a SiO2 layer. In more detail, oxidation produces a SiO2-rich glass layer, a subsurface oxide layer, and a MoB, ZrO2, and SiO2 mixture layer [26].
In this work, the possibility of producing graded composites with a fibre gradient ranging from 0 to 50 vol% via hot pressing sintering was investigated. Three UHTC matrices were mixed with carbon fibres:
- ZrB2 -10 vol% SiC - 5 vol% Y2O3 where SiC is the oxidation resistant phase and Y2O3 is the sintering aid [4,27].
- ZrB2 -15 vol% MoSi2 where MoSi2 is both sintering and oxidation resistant aid [28,29].

5. Densification, microstructure and mechanical properties
As discussed in the previous section, various sintering techniques (both conventional and advanced) have been used to densify transition metal di-boride based materials, with the monolithic di-borides usually necessitating very high sintering temperatures. The reduction of starting powders particle size and the use of sinter-additives are possibly the major approaches that are used to address this issue (see Figs. 7 and 9). Both metallic and non-metallic sinter-additives have been used to obtain fully dense di-boride ceramics at low sintering temperatures. While selecting the sinter-additive, the type and amount must be judiciously chosen as they influence densification, microstructure, phase assemblage and properties of the di-borides. Metallic additives (generally < 2 wt% added to borides) such as Ni, Cu, Co, Cr and Fe etc. are advantageous towards obtaining near-theoretical sinter-density, reducing the sintering temperature considerably due to liquid phase sintering and very effective in improving room temperature mechanical properties of borides [5,9,10,50,204]. However, discussion on the use of metallic sinter-additives is not included here since the addition of metallic additives degrades the high temperature properties of the di-boride ceramics, eventually rendering them unsuitable for the ultra-high temperature applications. For example, the strength of 4 wt% Ni added ZrB2 increased from 370 to 630 MPa with increasing temperature (up to 800 °C) and further increasing the temperature resulted drastic reduction in strength (240 MPa at 1000 °C and 20 MPa at 1200 °C) [10]. Hence, the effects of non-metallic sinter-additives on the densification and microstructure of the important di-boride ceramics have been discussed in the following sub-sections.

12.4  SUMMARY
Tantalum carbides comprise a class of UHTC with a range of melting temperatures and phases dependent on the C/Ta ratio. The phase structures and precipitation sequence of these  materials  are  paramount  in  regulating  the  microstructure  morphologies. Microstructures can range from equiaxed grains to equiaxed grains with secondary laths in a crisscross pattern to acicular grains with secondary laths all parallel to the major axis of the grain. These microstructures have been shown to regulate the mechanical responses, particularly increasing the fracture toughness to approximately 13 MPa√m with a high volume fraction of Ta 4 C 3 . Most of the mechanical property work has been done on the monocarbide TaC phase. The mechanical behavior of this carbide shows a range of responses, dependent upon carbon content, porosity of the test specimen, and the testing means or environment. Because of these issues, reported mechanical data show variations. For example, agreement can be found in certain areas, such as decreasing hardness with decreasing carbon content below C/Ta = 0.8, whereas other tests show wide variations, including the reported activation energies in creep experiments. This demonstrates the challenges in studying these classes of materials. It is imperative that any conclusions based on literature reports carefully consider the intrinsic and extrinsic characteristics  of  the  tantalum  carbide  sample  tested.  Regardless  of  these  issues,  the range of mechanical properties with corresponding varied microstructures offers several exciting opportunities to tailor phases together that couple ultra-high melting temperature capability with metallic-like and ceramic-like mechanical responses.

4.2. Strengthening mechanism of heat-treated samples
The reduction in γ ′ ′ strengthening and the observed discrepancies in dislocation strengthening can be attributed to the presence of undissolved secondary phases, such as Ni5Hf, (Hf,Nb,Ti)C, and Laves phases, at grain boundaries in the HfCp-doped samples. In the 0HfC sample, γ ′ ′ formation and grain growth are enhanced by the dissolution of the Laves phase  and  the  release  of  dislocation  energy  during  heat  treatment. However, in HfCp-doped samples, these undissolved secondary phases consume Nb, which is crucial for γ ′ ′ formation. As a result, the volume fraction  of γ ′ ′ decreases  with  increasing  HfCp  content,  limiting  its contribution  to  strengthening  while  enhancing  dislocation  strengthening through interactions with these secondary phases.

trade-off between low-temperature sintering and good high-temperature strength.
In order to simultaneously achieve low-temperature preparation and good high-temperature resistance, it is needed to remove the residual ZrSi2 from the final ZrB2-based ceramic. In the previous studies [32,33], we found that introducing carbonaceous material into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  can reduce the amount of residual ZrSi2  in the final materials, due to the reaction between carbonaceous material and sintering additive of ZrSi2. However, the effects of sintering additive transformation on the densification  process,  phase  composition,  microstructure  and  mechanical properties are still unclear.
In the current study, active carbon was introduced into ZrB2 -ZrSi2 to prepare high-temperature-resistant ZrB2-based ceramics at a relatively low temperature via in situ reactive sintering. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 powders  were  hot-pressed  at  different  sintering  temperatures.  Phase composition, microstructure, densification behavior and hightemperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs were systematically investigated, in order to clarify the effects of active carbon on the densification and high-temperature properties of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
In this work, ZrB2 composites were produced containing high volumes, 40 vol%, of short carbon fiber plus 10 vol% of oxidation retardant phases. The latter consisted of 5 vol% of SiC combined with 5 vol% of one of three different MeSi2 compounds, viz. MoSi2, HfSi2 or WSi2. The selection of these silicides was based on their compatibility with high-temperature environments, i.e. they offer melting points above 1600 °C, high stiffness and, owing to their beneficial effects on densification, high temperature strength and potential oxidation resistance improvement for borides [23,25-28]. Additionally, a comparative material without MeSi2 but containing the same total amount of SH additives, i.e. 10 vol% SiC, was also prepared as a reference baseline. The question posed by this work was whether the replacement of 5 vol% of SiC with 5 vol% of MeSi2 would significantly improve the ablation resistance. Thus the UHTCMCs were exposed to the flame of an oxyacetylene torch (OAT) at temperatures above 2000 °C for 60 s. The analysis of the as-sintered and oxidized materials is presented and discussed, with the focus being on the effect of the secondary phases in promoting the formation of an ablation resistant microstructure.
The novelty of this work lies in the production and successful testing of a new class of UHTCMCs that can survive extreme conditions of oxidation at up to 2000 °C.

4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
One study by Hu et al. [187] commented on the mechanical properties as a function of ZrB2 content in the carbon-reinforced SiC matrix, indicating that the composite ' s flexural strength and fracture toughness decreased with increasing amounts of ZrB2. According to the report, the respective values of strength and toughness dropped from 367 MPa to 23.6 MPa m ½ without any boride to 163 MPa and 8.1 MPa m ½ when the matrix contained 60 vol% ZrB2. Their reasoning was that the boride phase  tended  to  react  at  the  fiber-matrix  interface,  causing  the  mechanical properties of the composite to degrade. Similar trends were observed in later studies involving carbide UHTCs. Chen et al. [191] investigated the effects of adding TaC to a Cf/SiC composite, while Yan et  al.  did  the  same  for  ZrC  [193]  and  HfC  [194].  In  all  three  cases, flexural strength decreased with increasing UHTC volume fraction, and the fracture toughness of the Cf/SiC -UHTC composites was lower than the Cf/SiC baseline. These results indicated a need for process refinement, given the preference for higher volume fractions of the UHTC phase due to superior oxidation resistance.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ =6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously. 10 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, as shown in previous work 10 and Fig. 1, are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density); (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.

The latter consisted of 5 vol% of SiC combined with 5 vol% of one of three different MeSi2 compounds, viz. MoSi2, HfSi2 or WSi2. At high temperature, the differences noticed at room temperature were generally flattened, but in general, carbides showed higher decrease rate of strength with the test temperature as compared to the borides, despite the tests were conducted in partially  protective  Argon  environment.  Both  at  1200  and  1500°C,  the  strength  values of borides containing TaSi 2 were higher than borides containing MoSi 2 . Excellent strength values at 1500°C were obtained for HfB 2 -based composites, 550-600 MPa, retaining the 75-86% of the room temperature values. This outstanding behavior was attributed to the highest refractoriness of HfB 2 over the other matrices and probably also to the formation of highly refractory silico-borides at the triple junctions which hindered the grain boundary sliding.
This study comprises an overview of densification behavior and mechanical properties of several UHTC matrices and pointed out that UHTCs can be sintered at temperatures as low as 1700°C if the suitable sintering additive is employed. This outcome is encouraging if we intend to introduce secondary phases prone to degradation with temperature, such as fibers or nanotubes, in order to improve the fracture toughness, which remains the weakest point of UHTCs.
A good  balance  between  room  temperature  mechanical  properties  and  high temperature behavior can be achieved with proper tuning of production process and tailoring of the constituent phases, focusing on the final conditions that the material will have to face in terms of stress state, temperatures, oxidizing/reducing environment.

question: What elastic (Young's) modulus values have been reported across these UHTC studies, for materials ranging from a Cf/C-UHTC composite to the ZrB2 phase and carbon-fiber reinforcement used in UHTC composites?
answer:
Elastic (Young's) modulus values reported across these UHTC studies include:

- For the ZrB2 phase, values range from 340 GPa to 582 GPa, with a specific value of 500 GPa noted for ZrB2 ceramic [23][7][13].
- For carbon-fiber reinforcement in UHTC composites, the Young's modulus of the carbon fiber is reported as 780 GPa [10].
- For Cf/ZrB2 composites, the Young's modulus after pyrolysis and sintering was found to be 43 GPa and 49 GPa, respectively [16].
- For a Cf - ZrB2 - SiC composite, the Young's modulus is reported as 75 GPa [26].

The elastic (Young's) modulus values for the Cf/C-UHTC composite are not covered in the provided sources. Similarly, the elastic (Young's) modulus values for the ZrB2 phase in UHTC composites and for carbon-fiber reinforcement in UHTC composites are partially covered, as noted above.

4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
Investigations of continuous carbon reinforcements for UHTC-based materials first gained traction in the early 2000s. A study by A. Sayir [184] attempted to use chemical vapor technology to deposit HfC and TaC onto a 2D carbon fabric weave. Although the carbide was incorporated more like a coating than an infiltrated matrix, the investigation represented  an  early  success  at  demonstrating  the  compatibility  between a UHTC material and continuous carbon reinforcement. The authors  of  the  study  alluded  to  the  expected  structural  benefits  of employing a 3D architecture over the 2D fabric that was used at the time. Subsequent studies [185 -192] looked at introducing UHTC phases into composite architectures, although only in small amounts. Initially, it was more strategic to work with systems that were primarily Cf/C or M. Ammendola et al.

As such, the UHTC phase was treated as an additive of limited volume percent, with the matrix phase still primarily consisting of carbon or SiC. The microstructure of one such composite can be seen Fig. 18, which depicts ZrB2 particles in a SiC matrix with carbon fiber reinforcement. About 4 vol% of ZrB2 particles were infiltrated into a carbon fiber preform, followed by CVI processes that introduced PyC and SiC as the matrix chemistries.

3.2 Development of analytical model
ZrB 2 is known to be the major component in UHTCs, which is anticipated to have a high influence. However, owing to the majority of systems containing a high volume of ZrB2 -resulting in limited variability in the concentration-the result in Figure 4 demonstrates it to be the less influential variable. Only a few UHTC systems contain Si3 N4 , HfC, ZrC, and TaSi 2 , and these systems show a small variation on Young's modulus. Therefore, those input variables demonstrate the least influences. It is critical to understand that the influences of input variables are evaluated from a relatively small database. Although the database in this study is much larger than previous UHTC studies, it is still small compared to other ML databases (containing thousands of data-records). A much more comprehensive database may enhance the influence of inconsequential input variables.

1. Introduction
In view of the advantages and disadvantages of these two kinds of materials, researchers have conducted many exploratory researches to design new composites in order to meet specific ultrahigh temperature application requirements. Carbon fibers are currently the only suitable reinforcement for use at ultra-high temperatures up to 3000 °C, with high specific strength, high specific modulus, high stiffness, and low coefficient of thermal expansion. Simultaneously, they can readily be formed into a construct or preform of desired configuration by winding, weaving, knitting, braiding, or wrapping over a suitably formed mandrel. Therefore, a composite with carbon fiber as reinforcement and UHTC or C-SiCUHTC as matrix can be expected to perform good oxidation and ablation resistance properties at ultra-high temperatures compared to C/C and C/SiC composites, as well as good fracture toughness, thermal shock resistance and damage tolerance compared to UHTCs, and then to be the potential candidates for use at ultra-high temperatures.
In the past decade, many investigations associated with carbon fiber reinforced UHTC and C-SiC-UHTC matrix composites have been reported. In the view of material design, the reports are mainly focused on interfacial structure, preform configuration, and matrix composition, structure and distribution. In the view of material fabrication, the researchers have developed a series of techniques to introduce UHTCs into carbon fiber composites, mainly containing chemical vapor infiltration/deposition (CVI/D), precursor infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), reactive melt infiltration (RMI), slurry infiltration (SI), in-situ reaction and hot pressing (HP). In the view of material properties, the researchers are most interested in mechanical, and oxidation- and ablation-resistant properties of these composites. The purpose of this paper is to give an overview of the state of the art in the design, preparation and properties of carbon fiber reinforced UHTC or C-SiC-UHTC composites for ultra-high temperature aerospace application during the last decade.

A B S T R A C T
A process technology for the processing of the continuous carbon fibre reinforced ZrB2  (Cf -ZrB2) ultra high temperature ceramic matrix composite (UHTCMC) has been developed. The processing steps are slurry infiltration followed by curing and pyrolysis. The composite was then subjected to high temperature pressureless sintering.  The  microstrcutural  and  mechanical  properties  of  Cf -ZrB2  UHTCMC  were  characterized  at  the completion of each processing step. The de-lamination between the carbon fibre and the ZrB2 matrix layer in 2D Cf -ZrB2 composite after pressureless sintering was found. However, the problem of de-lamination after sintering was not detected in the 2.5D Cf -ZrB2 composite by stitching the infiltrated layers in the Z-direction. Significant improvements in the sintered density, Young ' s modulus and flexural strength were achieved through 2.5D Cf -ZrB2  composite.

3.2. Effect of fiber distribution on the strengths of SCf/UHTCMCs
In the model studied, the modulus of elasticity for the fibers is lower than that of the matrix material, with fiber pull-out not accounted for in this  analysis. Consequently, as the strain on the composite increases, damage initiates within the matrix material prior to any significant fiber failure. Introducing a certain proportion of carbon fibers to augment the toughness  of  Ultra-High  Temperature  Ceramic  Composites  paradoxically  results  in  a  reduction  in  the  composite ' s  overall  strength.  This phenomenon is attributed  to  the  fibers ' modulus  being  substantially lower in the radial direction compared to the axial direction. Therefore, enhancing the radial distribution of fibers contributes to an increase in the composite ' s overall strength.
To ascertain the reliability of the influence of fiber distribution on the  calculated  tensile  strength,  this  study  conducts  a  comparative analysis based on the experimental results of random short carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2-SiC composites, as reported in the literature [33,34]. Given the pronounced variability in the mechanical properties of short carbon  fiber  reinforced  ZrB2-SiC  composites  due  to  the  fabrication process,  this  analysis  primarily  focuses  on  assessing  the  extent  of strength  reduction  attributable  to  variations  in  fiber  volume  fraction within the composite material. Data from Case 5, characterized by a 3D random fiber distribution, were selected for this comparative analysis. The findings of this comparison are presented in Fig. 13.

A B S T R A C T
A study has been carried out to examine the influence of augmented intra-bundle spacing achieved through innovative pre-treatment (heat treatment or ultrasonication) of carbon fibre (Cf) fabric, on mechanical properties of Cf-ZrB2-SiC composites processed by ZrB2 -SiC slurry infiltration and hot pressing. Significantly enhanced Cf fabric intra-bundle spacing has facilitated homogeneous slurry infiltration, but has lowered elastic modulus, flexural strength, and fracture toughness compared to the composite with as-received Cf, because of fibre disorientation introduced during pre-treatment and its partial degradation by formation of interfacial ZrC during sintering. Notably, the composite with heat-treated Cf has shown superior fracture toughness (7.78 ± 0.4 MPa √ m) and work of fracture (3043.25 ± 24.2 J/m 2 ) compared to that containing ultra-sonicated Cf by 52.5 % and 95.7 %, respectively. Despite these variations, all the composites have exhibited non-catastrophic fracture behaviour during testing owing to the role of fibre pull-out, crack bridging, and crack deflection operating as toughening mechanisms.

Ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites ( UHTCMCs ) based on ZrB2-matrix reinforced with 45 vol% of unidirectional continuous carbon /uniFB01 bers are studied through the thermal mechanical hysteresis in order to investigate the thermal damage accumulation. The analysis carried out allowed to extrapolate the Young's modulus of the matrix from thermal expansion measures. Onthe other hand, the analysis suggested that carbon /uniFB01 bers keep their Young's modulus constant at 780 GPa. 4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
Beyond the traditional UHTC -SiC systems, a number of unconventional  materials  systems  have  been  looked  at  to  enhance  the  matrix phase in continuous fiber UHTCMCs. Yaghobizadeh et al. [202] incorporated the MAX phase Ti3SiC2 into a Cf/ZrC -SiC, as MAX materials can exhibit some of the same properties as metals, including ductility and thermal shock resistance. The fracture toughness of the composite was tested to be 16.9 ± 0.6 MPa m ½ , which is among the highest metrics reported in literature for UHTCMC materials. Additionally, Ti3SiC2 also has a reported melting temperature higher than 3000 ◦ C, making it a candidate for ultra-high temperature applications with further testing and validation of good oxidation behavior. Another novel matrix phase inclusion are the rare earth oxides, which were studied in two papers [203,204]  by  Vinci  et  al.  A  series  of  Cf/ZrB2 -SiC  composites  were manufactured with 5 vol% of different rare earth additives (Y2O3, Sc2O3, La2O3,  Ce2O3).  Aside  from  the  composite  containing  Ce2O3 -which degraded  in  ambient  atmosphere -all  the  composites  showed  improvements  in  high  temperature  flexural  strength  ( > 600  MPa)  and fracture toughness ( > 10 MPa m ½ ) compared to the baseline Cf/ZrB2 -SiC without rare earth additives. To obtain further insight into the mechanical behavior of the joined UHTCs, the distribution of elastic modulus at the interface was investigated. Figure 5C shows the distribution of storage modulus in TaC-  HfC joint ceramic, obtained from the scanned area of 5 × 5 μm 2 .  For acquiring an elastic response such as modulus, the imposed load was maintained at a low value of 1.0 μN. For such studies, the surface needs to be extremely flat, which was not possible in the present case of UHTCs (see Figures 5B and 3). The modulus map distribution elicits microstructural heterogeneity, that is, sensitivity  to  the  topography,  shape,  pores,  and  composition. However, similar to the microstructure (Figure 3), modulus distribution  clearly  distinguishes  the  interface  (see  Figure 5C;  i.e.,  higher  modulus  on  the  TaC  side  indicated  by  red color and lower modulus values towards HfC side, marked by purple color). This distribution of the modulus around the interface  separating  it  from  two  UHTC parts also supports that the solid-  state diffusion has taken place to form a joint. It is essential to mention here that the absolute modulus measured at  the  rough  locations  is  extremely  low  compared  to that  of  the  actual  values  for  UHTCs.  This  skews  the  color gradient in the map, making it challenging to visualize the distribution. Hence, the color map's quantitative gradient is normalized to a quantitative one according to the following protocol. At each interface, the highest and lowest measured elastic moduli are designated 1 and 0, respectively. The distribution of moduli between these extremes is divided into four equal quarters, as presented in Figure 5C.

A B S T R A C T
reinforcement [6] and continuous carbon fiber reinforcement [7]. The mechanical properties of UHTCs and their composites have also been characterized and evaluated systematically. A series of temperature-dependent  mechanical  properties  prediction  models  for UHTCs have been proposed [8]. The influence of fiber/matrix interface for the mechanical properties of fiber-reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic  matrix  composites  has  also  been  investigated  [9].  However, although the incorporation of toughening phases can improve the fracture toughness and damage tolerance of UHTCs, the existence of the toughening phase may cause agglomeration, CTE mismatch, or difficulty in densification of the composites, leading to an increase in defects and a decrease  in  the  strength  of  the  composites  [10,11].  Therefore,  it  is difficult to keep balance between strength and toughness.
Among these toughening methods, the 3D continuous carbon fiber reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (3D Cf/ UHTCs), which have a 3D continuous carbon fiber skeleton to maximize the toughening effect of the carbon fibers, represent the most efficient approach for overcoming the intrinsic brittleness of UHTCs [2]. However, while the toughness of UHTCs can be improved, the interlaced carbon fiber skeleton also hinders the integration of UHTCs with carbon fiber preforms, leading to insufficient content of UHTCs, which makes it difficult for the composites to form a dense and continuous oxide layer during ablation. The loose oxide layer structure causes a weak bonding strength between the oxide layer and the matrix, making the oxide layer easier to peel off under the erosion of gas flow [2]. Thus, efficiently integrating 3D continuous carbon fiber preforms with UHTCs to acquire high-performance  composites  has  become  one  of  the  most  serious challenges in recent years, which has continued to gain the attention of researchers worldwide but remains unresolved.

A B S T R A C T
Manufacturing of complex-shaped UHTCs occasionally requires joining of dissimilar materials. Herein diffusion bonded (1800 ◦ C and 40 MPa) two dissimilar composites, ZrB2-20vol%SiC (ZS) and HfB2-20vol% SiC (HS), are compared with similar ones i.e., ZS-ZS. The ZS-ZS joints exhibit a seamless interface with uniform hardness (~20 GPa) and Young ' s Modulus (~366 GPa). For ZS-HS, diffusion and reactivity between Hf and Zr created an interface with (Zr, Hf)B2  solid solution, thus, decreasing interfacial hardness and Young ' s modulus (by ~17 %, ~3 %) from HS sides, but were similar to that of ZS side. Under compressive stresses, both joints exhibited interfacial  stability  and  comparable  bending  and  shear  strength,  i.e.,  (~259  MPa,  ~87  MPa  for  ZS-HS,  and ~296 MPa, ~113 MPa for ZS-ZS). Though good thermal stability of joints is observed when oxidized at 1400 ◦ C, 60 min, ZS-ZS joints with uniform mechanical properties and good structural and thermal stability are more promising for complex shape applications.
SiC and HfB2  with 20 vol% SiC are recognized as baseline composite systems for elevated temperature (~1600 ◦ C) thermal protection applications  [16].  Nevertheless,  the  practical  application  of  these  composites  is  restricted  by  the  significant  challenge  of  fabricating  large, intricately  shaped  items,  and  therefore,  joining  these  composites  becomes increasingly essential.

4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
One study by Hu et al. [187] commented on the mechanical properties as a function of ZrB2 content in the carbon-reinforced SiC matrix, indicating that the composite ' s flexural strength and fracture toughness decreased with increasing amounts of ZrB2. According to the report, the respective values of strength and toughness dropped from 367 MPa to 23.6 MPa m ½ without any boride to 163 MPa and 8.1 MPa m ½ when the matrix contained 60 vol% ZrB2. Their reasoning was that the boride phase  tended  to  react  at  the  fiber-matrix  interface,  causing  the  mechanical properties of the composite to degrade. Similar trends were observed in later studies involving carbide UHTCs. Chen et al. [191] investigated the effects of adding TaC to a Cf/SiC composite, while Yan et  al.  did  the  same  for  ZrC  [193]  and  HfC  [194].  In  all  three  cases, flexural strength decreased with increasing UHTC volume fraction, and the fracture toughness of the Cf/SiC -UHTC composites was lower than the Cf/SiC baseline. These results indicated a need for process refinement, given the preference for higher volume fractions of the UHTC phase due to superior oxidation resistance.

5.3. Mechanical properties
The primary mechanical properties required for analyses of isotropic UHTC components are the tensile modulus and the Poisson's ratio. Both appear as first-order terms in the governing static equations. For isotropic materials, the shear modulus, G , can be calculated from the tensile modulus and Poisson's ratio as G = E /[2(1 + ν ) and does not have to be independently specified.
Thetensile modulus (also called the Young's modulus or elastic modulus) has a large effect on the mechanical response of the component. Ideally, tensile modulus values are derived from the slope (or first derivative) of a material's stress-strain curve under uniaxial tension in the elastic loading regime. However UHTC materials have very limited ductility, and their tensile moduli are almost always either measured by acoustic methods or derived from experimental stress-strain curves obtained during flexure testing. The flexure method was found to produce somewhat lower (by ∼ 15%) values for some UHTC materials than the tensile and acoustic methods. 3,56
Tensile modulus values can be used directly in mechanical analyses, but it is often more accurate to incorporate the actual stress-strain relationship. Most analytical software packages can handle inputs of either type. Incorporating the experimental stress-strain curve is particularly advantageous for materials that exhibit non-linear elastic response or for when the experimental stress-strain curve contains plastic strain contributions deemed important for a particular application. Non-linear, elastic deformation behaviors are observed for some UHTC materials at elevated temperatures. 3,40-42,57 The tensile modulus magnitude tends to decrease with increasing temperature. Accurate temperature-dependent modulus data are desirable, but difficult to measure. In practice, if the expected thermal and load environments combine to push a UHTC component into a highly non-linear or highly temperature-dependent mechanical property regime, a redesign of the component or system is likely.

A B S T R A C T
reinforcement [6] and continuous carbon fiber reinforcement [7]. The mechanical properties of UHTCs and their composites have also been characterized and evaluated systematically. A series of temperature-dependent  mechanical  properties  prediction  models  for UHTCs have been proposed [8]. The influence of fiber/matrix interface for the mechanical properties of fiber-reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic  matrix  composites  has  also  been  investigated  [9].  However, although the incorporation of toughening phases can improve the fracture toughness and damage tolerance of UHTCs, the existence of the toughening phase may cause agglomeration, CTE mismatch, or difficulty in densification of the composites, leading to an increase in defects and a decrease  in  the  strength  of  the  composites  [10,11].  Therefore,  it  is difficult to keep balance between strength and toughness.
Among these toughening methods, the 3D continuous carbon fiber reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (3D Cf/ UHTCs), which have a 3D continuous carbon fiber skeleton to maximize the toughening effect of the carbon fibers, represent the most efficient approach for overcoming the intrinsic brittleness of UHTCs [2]. However, while the toughness of UHTCs can be improved, the interlaced carbon fiber skeleton also hinders the integration of UHTCs with carbon fiber preforms, leading to insufficient content of UHTCs, which makes it difficult for the composites to form a dense and continuous oxide layer during ablation. The loose oxide layer structure causes a weak bonding strength between the oxide layer and the matrix, making the oxide layer easier to peel off under the erosion of gas flow [2]. Thus, efficiently integrating 3D continuous carbon fiber preforms with UHTCs to acquire high-performance  composites  has  become  one  of  the  most  serious challenges in recent years, which has continued to gain the attention of researchers worldwide but remains unresolved.

A B S T R A C T
Different Hf/Zr-based UHTCs modified C/C composites were fabricated via the reactive melt infiltration (RMI) adopting HfSi2, ZrSi2, and HfSi2-ZrSi2  hybrid alloys with different molar ratios (3:1, 1:1, 1:3). The microstructural evolution of the composites, variation of mechanical properties, and ablation performance of the composites in the absence of in-situ coating were studied. The C/HfC-ZrC-SiC composites were all divided into the exterior region with more UHTC phases and the interior region with more SiC and silicides, and the UHTC-rich region enlarged with the HfSi2 content increased in the RMI alloy. The flexural strengths of C/HfC-ZrC-SiC were enhanced to 238.37 ± 20.42 Mpa and 237.26 ± 17.57 Mpa when adopting HfSi2-ZrSi2 alloys with molar ratios of 1:1 and 1:3. As the HfSi2  content in the RMI alloy increased, the oxide layer became compact with sintering degree increased, enhancing the ablation resistance of the composites and reducing the mass ablation rate.

3.2.1 Elastic modulus and flexural strength
The elastic modulus and flexural strength of the Cf -ZrB 2 -SiC composites prepared with different fiber architectures are reported in Table 2. Among the prepared composites, the UD-0/0 ◦ composite has exhibited the highest elastic modulus value ( ∼ 153.1 GPa), when measured along the fiber orientation. In contrast, the elastic moduli of the UD-0/90 ◦ and 8H-SW composites are found to be less compared to the UD-0/0 ◦ by ∼ 41.6% and ∼ 100%, respectively. In other words, the 8H-SW composite has exhibited the lowest elastic modulus among the fiber architectures investigated in this study. These variations in the experimentally determined values of Young's modulus may be explained by considering that the fibers oriented only along the loading direction of the test (longitudinal direction) contribute to the measured elastic constants. The anisotropy of C f is due to the elastic modulus being very high along the fiber axis, but low in the transverse direction. 1 Such anisotropy of elastic modulus is found in the C-fibers due to the strong covalent C-C bonds in the graphite structure being aligned along the fiber axis, and the presence of relatively weak interatomic (van der Waals) forces between the basal planes along the transverse direction. 55 In both UD-0/90 ◦ and 8H-SW composites, a significant fraction of the fibers have transverse orientation with respect to the test direction, and therefore has not contributed to the estimated value of Young's modulus. It should be noted that 50% of the total number of C f in the UD-0/90 ◦ fabric has a transverse orientation (i.e., five out of 10 laminae possess 90 ◦ orientation) with respect to the loading axis, while each layer of the C f fabric in the 8H-SW preform consists of both 0 ◦ and 90 ◦ -oriented fibers forming a mini-composite lamina.

question: What activation energy values were determined for the densification and sintering kinetics of UHTC ceramics?
answer:
The activation energy values for the densification and sintering kinetics of UHTC ceramics are as follows:

- For the intermediate stage sintering of HfB2, the activation energy (QD) is approximately 888 kJ/mol [7].
- For the final stage sintering of HfB2, the activation energy (Qg) is approximately 475 kJ/mol [7].
- For the densification of HfC-TiC ceramics, the oxidation activation energy is 385.39 kJ/mol [4].
- For the densification of ZrB2 ceramics during the final stage, the activation energy is approximately 168 ± 24 kJ/mol [19].
- For the grain growth of the HEB phase, the activation energy is estimated to be 317 kJ/mol [11].
- For the densification of ZrB2, the activation energy for grain-boundary diffusion is 2070 kJ/mol [9].

These values reflect the different mechanisms and conditions under which densification and sintering occur in UHTC ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
This study investigates the sintering kinetics during the intermediate and final stages of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of HfB2  over a temperature range of 1750 -1950 ◦ C under uniaxial pressure of 50 MPa. During the intermediate stage (T < 1850 ◦ C), a creep deformation-based model was utilised for studying the densification kinetics.  The  evaluated stress  exponent (n) ~1  and an activation energy ( QD )  of  ~888 kJ/mol suggest lattice diffusion  to  be  the  dominant  densification  mechanism  during  this  stage.  At  1850 ◦ C,  a  transition  to  grain boundary sliding was observed, with n ~1.5, as confirmed by microstructural analysis. In the final stage of sintering (T > 1850 ◦ C), a grain growth-based model with grain growth exponent ( m ) ~ 3 and an activation energy ( Qg ) of ~475 kJ/mol supported the dominance of lattice diffusion during grain growth. At 1850 ◦ C, a relative  density  of  ~97  %  was  achieved,  marking  a  shift  from  interface-controlled  deformation  via  grain boundary sliding to lattice diffusion-driven grain growth. Thus, this study highlights the sintering mechanisms of HfB2  ceramics at various SPS temperatures, plausibly aiding in microstructure optimization for improved performance of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) in extreme environments.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), owing to their exceptional performance under extreme heat and harsh conditions, have become the preferred choice for critical thermal protection materials in high-speed aerospace vehicles. Here, the HfC-TiC ceramics were fabricated through pressureless sintering, and its oxidation behaivor was  investigated  using  non-isothermal  method.  The  oxidation  activation  energy  of  HfC-TiC  sample  reaches 385.39 kJ/mol. Then the ablation behavior of HfC-TiC ceramics was tested under plasma ablation conditions (3000 K, 5.0 MW/m 2 ). The mass ablation rate and linear ablation rate of HfC-TiC can reach GLYPH<0> 1.11 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3  g ⋅ s GLYPH<0> 1 and GLYPH<0> 1.40 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3  mm ⋅ s GLYPH<0> 1 after  being  ablated  for  300 seconds,  respectively.  The  oxide  layers  in  the  center consisted of a porous oxide upper layer, a dense Ti-enriched intermediate layer, and an oxide inner layer. This complex multi-layered oxidative configuration synergistically protected the underlying carbide matrix, significantly enhancing the ablation resistance of HfC-TiC ceramics.

The oxidation product of SiC, SiO2, forms a liquid phase at high temperatures, effectively sealing pores and cracks,  and  creating  a  dense  Hf-Si-O  oxidation  layer  [14,15].  This significantly improves the oxidation and ablation resistance of the material. However, under low oxygen partial pressure, a large amount of SiC is depleted by active oxidation, ultimately resulting in the formation of a porous layer [16,17]. Additionally, the SiO2  phase rapidly volatilizes  in  environments  exceeding  2000 ℃ ,  thereby  expediting  the decomposition of the oxide layer [18].

A B S T R A C T
Despite  advancements  in  sintering  techniques,  understanding  of sintering kinetics and grain growth mechanisms in spark plasma sintered HfB2 remains limited, particularly under varying sintering conditions. Kalish et al. [21], provided valuable insights into the final stage densification during high-pressure hot-pressing of fine HfB2 particles at 1400 -1900 ◦ C  through  analysis  based  on  the  Nabarro-Herring  creep model. The densification (~98.6 %) is driven by stress-directed vacancy diffusion, with an activation energy of ~95.81 kJ/mol. The rate-controlling process  involves either  interstitial  lattice  diffusion  of boron or grain boundary diffusion of hafnium, while initial densification occurs through particle fragmentation and rearrangement. This creep-based estimation of the densification mechanism has been successfully applied to various ceramics and intermetallics [23 -29].

A B S T R A C T
Entropy-stabilized Ultra High-Temperature Ceramics (UHTC) offer a groundbreaking solution to the challenges of  extreme  environments,  showcasing  enhanced  mechanical  properties,  thermal  stability,  and  resistance  to oxidation  at  high  temperatures.  The  consolidation  of  UHTC  by  ultra-fast  high-temperature  sintering  (UHS) significantly reduces processing times and temperature and can produce dense high-performance ceramics with superior mechanical properties. This study reports the pressureless synthesis and consolidation of the entropystabilized  (Hf0.25Zr0.25Ti0.25V0.25)B2-B4C  composite  through  UHS  within  1  minute,  starting  from  transition metal diboride powders. B4C acts as an effective sintering aid, promoting the densification of the system and the formation of a nearly single-phase hexagonal diboride with a diboride-eutectic phase. Furthermore, a secondary minor hexagonal phase rich in V and Zr is formed close to the eutectic regions. Sintering currents of 40 A were necessary to reach densities higher than 90 % under pressureless conditions, achieving nano hardness higher than 27.3 GPa, comparable with high-entropy diborides produced by Spark Plasma Sintering. The study highlights the entropy-stabilized phase formation, diffusion, densification, and grain growth mechanisms involved during UHS. The work contributes to the understanding of entropy-stabilized ceramics produced by UHS as a faster and less energy-consuming process than conventional sintering methods.

If D 2 is less than D 1 then a reduction in the sintering temperature is predicted (i.e., T 2 < T 1). This is indeed the trend observed experimentally. Unfortunately, the activation energy of the grain-boundary diffusion in ZrB2, responsible for its densification, cannot be inferred from the data of T OIS, T MSR, or T OFS because T OIS is reached in the sintering regime controlled by surface diffusion, T MSR is reached at different degrees of densification, and T OFS is reached in many cases during the regime of isothermal heating at 1900 ◦ C. This is a very high activation energy, which explains why the commercially-available ZrB2 powders with micrometre-sized particles are considered to be intrinsically unsinterable without sintering additives at moderate temperatures.

6.10  CONCLUSIONS
Although UHTCs are considered to be poorly sinterable compounds, multiple processing options are available to achieve fully dense ceramics with controlled microstructures. All the possible MB 2 /sintering additive combinations have not been tested for both ZrB 2 and HfB 2 , but a tentative rationalization has been devised for the most important densification mechanisms. Densification can be accomplished by the following:
- Solid state sintering, in the presence of carbon, B 4 C, and WC, at temperatures between  1850  and  1950°C;  no  amorphous  phases  are  observed  along  grain boundaries.  Sintered  materials  are  very  refractory,  with  600  MPa  of  flexural strength up to 1500°C in air.
- Liquid-phase sintering in the presence of metals or Si 3 N 4 at temperatures generally lower than 1850°C for both ZrB 2 and HfB 2 .  Low-melting metallic phases, residual  Si-O-based  amorphous  phases,  and  low-melting  silicides  are  found along grain boundaries upon cooling. At temperatures greater than 1000°C, significant strength degradation is observed.
- Transient liquid-phase sintering, in the presence of MeSi 2 (Me = Zr, Mo, Ta, W), at temperatures 1700-1900°C. A special case is ZrSi 2 enabling densification at 1550°C. In all cases, formation of solid solutions and cation interdiffusion are
both mechanisms that help densification. By using ZrSi 2 ,  low-melting silicides are found at triple points, while amorphous grain boundaries are often observed by using TaSi 2 . In contrast, residual amorphous phases are rarely found in MB 2 -MeSi 2 composites  (Me = Mo, W), which retained strength values close to 600 MPa  at  1500°C  in  air,  with  appropriate  tuning  of  processing  and  sintering conditions.

In comparison, the sintering activation energy for (TiZrHfVNbTa)C was 839 ± 53 kJ/mol [52], also associated with lattice diffusion, while for TiC with  390 kJ/mol  [52] grain  boundary  diffusion  was  the  primary mechanism. In the present HEB phase, the densification process involves a solid-state grain boundary diffusion and material transport through a transient  liquid  phase,  which  takes  place  at  the  grain  boundaries, improving the ceramic densification.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have been proposed to serve as thermal protection system (TPS) materials for various applications at temperatures above 2000 °C, such as sharp leading edges and nose cones of next-generation hypersonic vehicles, rocket propulsion lining and advanced energy systems [1,2]. Among the UHTCs, ternary Ta x Hf 1-x C (0 < x < 1) ceramics have attracted worldwide attention in recent decades due to their ultra-high melting point (> 3700 °C) [3] Owing to the same crystal structure with NaCl ( B1 , space group Fm3m ), continuous Ta x Hf 1-x C (0 < x < 1) solid solution could be obtained with TaC and HfC across the whole compositional ranges [4].
Di /uniFB00 usion-driven mass transport mechanism contributes mostly to solid-state densi /uniFB01 cation of Ta x Hf 1-x C (0 < x < 1) ceramics [5]. Since the di /uniFB00 usion activation energy of HfC is higher (HfC: 60.42 kJ/mol, TaC: 39.49 kJ/mol) [6], TaC-rich solid solution carbide (Ta0.8Hf0.2C) is more cost-e /uniFB00 ective to be consolidated with commercially available TaC

3.5. Influence of reaction kinetics on reaction sintering of UHTC layers
To this point, much of the analysis of SLRS-UHTCs has been centered on the relationship between non-oxide yields and reaction thermodynamics  without  considering  kinetics.  Thorough  precursor  conversion does not singularly dictate the viability of SLRS for UHTC production and compatibility with powder bed fusion AM. The ability to synthesize numerous refractory ceramics on AM-relevant time scales is only significant for refractory component fabrication if interparticle adhesion can  be  achieved.  Traditional  PBF-AM  relies  on  non-reactive  binding mechanisms like full/partial melting or sintering that are not generally applicable to UHTCs due to their high melting temperatures and poor response to laser energy deposition. For UHTCs with very low diffusion coefficients,  reaction  energies  associated  with  synthesis  can  initiate atomic  mobility  for  interparticle  adhesion.  This  reaction  bonding concept may be applied for the simultaneous synthesis and robust layer formation through interparticle adhesion for AM [78].
Reaction  bonding  during  SLRS  is  based  on  gas-solid  (or  liquid) reactivity and a dynamically changing mixture of product reactants and intermediates.  Thermal  activation  is  necessary  for  reactivity,  atomic transport, and sintering-induced densification. A generalized approach

4.3.1. Mode of energy input
Sintering of UHTCs is either through a solid-state or a liquid phase process. Sintering, as well as EBS, works on the principle of application of thermal energy for powder consolidation. Heat is generally applied to the pressed powder through an induction furnace or resistance heating furnace. Graphite resistors are utilized for pressureless sintering, while hydraulic pressure is applied in the case of hot pressing. Spark Plasma Sintering applies graphite dies and plungers that conduct a pulsed direct current [38]. The heating is usually uniform throughout the powder, and the processes typically last a considerable time to enable densification.
On the other hand, the EBS processing for UHTCs uses a concentrated electron beam as a heat source. The accelerated electrons, which are traveling at about 70% of the speed of light, fall onto the powder bed resulting  in  a  conversion  of  kinetic  energy  to  thermal  energy.  The thermal energy is maintained in such a way that the sintering additive (ZrSi2) is melted, forming a liquid phase that binds the UHTC (ZrB2) grains together. There have already been attempts to sinter ZrB2 based UHTCs through the liquid phase sintering process using various doping agents or sintering additives [39]. However, such a process has not been explored so much using electron beam technology. In this research direction, stability of the melt pool and the control of the molten material are of prime importance, and that is achieved by carefully controlling the parameters that define the EBS process (beam power, beam scanning speed, preheat temperature, beam spot size, etc.)

1. Introduction
All the so-called ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs; i.e., borides, carbides, and nitrides of the early-transition metals [1]) have poor intrinsic sinterability due to the serious kinetic restrictions imposed by their strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coef /uniFB01 cients. This is further aggravated extrinsically because the starting powders normally comprise large particles of various microns in size and with surface oxide impurities. Not surprisingly, dedicated sintering studies constitute a large part of the research effort carried out on UHTCs, and particularly on ZrB2-based UHTCs [2,3]. They have enabled the identi /uniFB01 cation of appropriate sintering additives and comminution practices. Some of these suitable additives are carbon [4,5], B4C [6], WC [7,8], and B4C þ C [9,10], which favor the conventional solid-state sintering of

UHTCs by the carbothermal reduction of the surface metal oxides. This has been proven both for pressureless sintering (PS) and hot-pressing (HP), both of which apply prolonged sintering cycles (i.e., various hours). Apart from facilitating the conventional sintering, cleaning oxides off of the grain boundaries also results in improved high-temperature strength [5,8]. Furthermore, solid-state sintering additives are normally used synergistically with the wet-attrition milling of the starting powders because solid-state sinterability scales inversely with a power of the particle size (i.e., Herring's scaling law [11]).

3. Processing techniques for UHTCs
Effective sintering of UHTCs requires the initial powder to have high purity  (although  metallic  impurities  aid  in  the  densification  process, whereas oxide impurities promote grain growth and are detrimental for densification),  small  powder  particle  size,  narrow  particle-size  distribution, and low agglomeration factor [45]. The low self-diffusion coefficient and strong covalent bonds in UHTCs make sintering difficult [46]. Hot pressing (HP) has typically been employed to densify UHTCs [47 -53], while achieving 94 -97% densification in TaC-based composites sintered at 1800 -2300 ◦ C [50 -53], while 98 -100% densification is obtained in  ZrB2-  based  composites  upon  sintering  in  1600 -1870 ◦ C range [47,49]. To achieve full densification of ZrB2 -30ZrC -10SiC, the total sintering time is reduced from ~140 min in HP (with 3.7 vol % Si3N4 as a sintering aid) to ~23 min in SPS (without any sintering aid) [54].  With  HP  and  SPS,  complex  shapes  cannot  be  prepared,  which pushes utilization of pressureless sintering (with inherently poor mechanical properties attributed to poor densification). Pressureless sintering and hot pressing of ZrB2  have shown Vickers ' hardness of 14.5 GPa and 23 GPa, the elastic modulus of 454 GPa and 489 GPa, and flexural  strength  of  444  MPa  and  565  MPa, respectively  [55]. Other techniques to consolidate UHTCs are atmospheric plasma spraying [56], vacuum plasma spraying [8] and chemical vapor infiltration [57], etc. The pros and cons of these processing techniques towards enunciating microstructure  and  resulting  consolidation,  illustrated  in  Table

Production of UHTC Complex Shapes and Architectures
Pressureless sintering  (PLS) was  considered  quite  impossible  for  UHTCs  until  the 1980s. Densification can be improved using liquid phase formers or reactive agents (Guo, 2009). Refractory metals such as Ni, Fe, Co, Mo, Re, Cu and Cr (Cheng & Gadalla, 1996; Einarsrud et al., 1997; Khanra & Godkini, 2005; Mishra et al., 2002; Yan et al., 2006) as well as disilicide of transition metals, such as MoSi 2 and ZrSi 2 (Guo et al., 2008; Sciti et al., 2006b; Sciti et al., 2006c), have been experimented as liquid phase formers. Metal additions resulted more efficient when amounting to ≥ 2
wt% and when sintering was performed in argon atmosphere rather than in vacuum (Chech et al., 1965). These two conditions ensure respectively sufficient formation and continuous presence and action of a liquid phase (added metals can be lost in vacuum due to volatilization) (Guo, 2009). The action of metallic additives that influence sintering is related to a contraction of the ZrB 2 crystal lattice that affects the surface free energy and thus increases the driving force for densification. For example, additions of Mo decreased the energy of activation of the densification, due to its incorporation into a ZrB 2 solid solution (Kislui & Kuzenkova, 1966).

A B S T R A C T
SPS consolidation [34]. Stanfield et al. [29] provided additional evidence that final stage sintering of ZrB2 ceramics is predominantly controlled by dislocation motion, with activation energies ~ 168 ± 24 kJ/mol. Other studies on final stage sintering of TaB2 via SPS at 2050 ◦ C [36] and PS at 1800 -2275 ◦ C [22] reported high activation energy, Q ~ 1078 kJ/mol and ~1020 kJ/mol, respectively, although the actual mechanism was not specified. Collectively, these studies emphasize the complexity of densification  mechanisms  in  transition  metal  diboride  (TMB2)-based ceramics. Moreover, it suggests the intricate interplay of temperature, pressure, and compositional factors in determining the final material properties.
Although significant studies exist on the sintering kinetics of ZrB2 and TaB2, sintering kinetics for pure HfB2 are still less explored. Hence, the current work systematically investigates transition in sintering kinetics  during the intermediate stage and final stage (in the sintering temperatures of 1750 -1950 ◦ C)  on  the  densification,  microstructural evolution, and grain growth mechanisms of HfB2 ceramics during SPS. The study uses creep deformation models to explore grain boundary sliding and diffusion mechanisms at different sintering temperatures. It also estimates creep behaviour at these high temperatures.

4.2.1. Consolidation phenomenon
The active LPS time, which is of the order of a few milliseconds, is not sufficient  for  densification,  and  the  UHTC  parts  as  produced  by  EBS might  potentially  require  a  post-sintering  process.  Additionally,  our previous work [2] has reported from the 1D melt track experiments that the melt pool has formed networks instead of balling up at higher beam powers, as shown in Fig. 6.

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.exm.2025.01.001
To  achieve  the  densification  of  UHTCs,  researchers  have  continuously explored and optimized fabrication techniques. Early hightemperature  and  high-pressure  sintering  methods  have  evolved  into modern multi-phase approaches combining vapor-phase, liquid-phase, and  solid-phase  techniques.  These  include  pressure  sintering,  slurry impregnation,  precursor  infiltration  and  pyrolysis,  reactive  melt  infiltration,  chemical  vapor  deposition,  and  additive  manufacturing [12,25]. Simultaneously, to further improve the toughness of UHTCs, a variety  of  toughening  strategies  have  been  introduced.  These  have progressed from early particle toughening to advanced techniques utilizing  whiskers,  soft  phases,  short  fibers,  and  continuous  fibers.  By optimizing  these  toughening  mechanisms,  the  crack  resistance  and fracture toughness of UHTCs have been significantly enhanced, making them  more  reliable  under  extreme  thermal  and  stress  conditions [26-28]. As illustrated in Fig. 1, research on UHTC matrix composites began to emerge around 2005, focusing on the development of 2.5D-Cfreinforced HfC and 2.5D-Cf-reinforced ZrB2-SiC composites [10]. Since then,  UHTC  matrix  composites  have  sparked  significant  research  interest, leading to a steady and rapid growth in the number of related publications each year.

A B S T R A C T
These promising attributes have prompted investigations exploring the synthesis of TaC-HfC ternary carbides by both traditional sintering [22,23] and melting techniques [24]. Densification of these compounds has  proven  challenging,  as  effective  sintering  of  most  compounds generally occurs between 50 % and 75 % of the material ' s homologous temperature [25]. Obtaining high density products with conventional sintering processes typically requires the use of sintering additives such as  pure  metals,  borides,  carbides,  oxides,  or  disilicide  compounds  to improve the consolidation to near theoretical density [26,27]. However, these additives diminish the maximum operating temperature as they introduce  lower  melting  temperature  species  and  may  provide  additional failure mechanisms from phases detrimental to operation in harsh environments.  Therefore,  manufacturing  the  aforementioned  UHTCs into components with high-density, without the use of sintering aids or dopants, is highly desirable.

1. Introduction
Traditional  densification  of  UHTC  matrix  composites  normally  requires  towering  sintering  temperature  over 1800 � C and high pressure, or dozens of PIP circles around 1600 � C. Illuminated by realization of ferroconcrete, Cf/UHTC-SiC is prepared via a novel densification method and mechanism at 1300 � C without pressure, using liquid polycarbosilane to 50 nm SiC as sufficient supplementation to filling holes and ensuring a strong bonding between dispersive ZrC particles inside carbon fabric after slurry injection. The obtained Cf/ZrC-SiC not only achieves high density at 4.19 g/cm 3 , but also has robust strength and graceful toughness at 286 � 25 MPa and 11.12 � 1.25 MPa m 1/2 , meanwhile gains a level of ascension in work of fracture to 3046 J/m 2 .

4. Conclusions
Temperature-dependent  sintering  kinetics  of  HfB ₂ during  spark plasma sintering (SPS) is evaluated at intermediate and final sintering stages.  At  T < 1850 ◦ C  (i.e.,  1750 ◦ C  and  1800 ◦ C),  densification mechanisms dominate, which is characteristic of the intermediate stage of sintering. However, at 1850 ◦ C, grain boundary sliding also contributes to the densification process marked by an increase in the stress exponent n (~1.5). The calculated activation energy QD ~ 888 kJ/mol is a critical parameter, confirming the lattice diffusion-controlled nature of densification in the intermediate stage. At T > 1900 ◦ C densification threshold (~97 %) is reached, thereby entering the final stage of sintering, with grain growth exponent m ~3 , confirming lattice diffusion assisted  grain  growth  as  the  dominant  mechanism  ( R 2 ~0.904, and activation  energy QG  ~ 475  kJ/mol ) .  The  microstructural  evolution, evidenced  by  a  reduction  in  grain  boundary  porosity  (~98.26  % decrease in H1950) and an increase in grain size from H1750 (~5.5 ± 1.9 μ m) to H1950 (~34.5 ± 14.1 μ m), highlights the transition from early-stage sintering (T < 1750 ◦ C), dominated by surface diffusion and limited grain boundary movement, to intermediate (1750 -1850 ◦ C) and final  stages  (1850 -1950 ◦ C).  It  further  confirms  lattice  diffusion  and grain  boundary  sliding  to  be  the  primary  densification  mechanisms, with grain boundary sliding becoming particularly dominant at 1850 ◦ C, consistent with obtained high activation energy. This transition in the sintering  mechanism  from  intermediate  to  final  stage  highlights

question: What holding or dwell time was used during sintering of the ceramic materials?
answer:
The holding or dwell times used during sintering of the ceramic materials varied depending on the specific process and material. For example:

- In one study, the sintering schedule included isothermal holds at 600 °C and 1600 °C for 30 min in vacuum, followed by sintering to 2100 °C with a holding time of 2 h [1].
- Another study reported that the isothermal hold was extended to 30 min to ensure a coarse and homogeneous microstructure [3].
- For ZrB2-based materials, the highest densification rate was achieved with a dwell time of less than 15 min, but it was extended to 30 min to ensure a coarse and homogeneous microstructure [3].
- In another case, a dwell time of 20 min was used during sintering at 1800 °C under a pressure of about 150 MPa [4].
- For some materials, the dwell time was adjusted between 5 to 50 min to achieve a stable shrinkage plateau and good densification [8].
- In a different process, the dwell time was 10 min at 1900 °C during spark plasma sintering [17].
- For the partial sintering method, the dwell time was not explicitly mentioned, but the process involves sintering at a lower temperature to create a sintering neck [19].

These examples illustrate the variation in holding or dwell times used during sintering of ceramic materials.

2.1. Raw materials
The whole sintering schedule included external isothermal holds at 600 8 C and 1600 8 C for 30 min in vacuum (15 Pa), respectively, and then argon gas (1 atm) was then introduced into the furnace. The gelcast ceramic was then continually pressureless sintered to 2100 8 C with a holding time of 2 h.
Fig. 1. SEM of ZrB2-SiC powder containing B4C and C as sintering additives.
Fig. 2. Flow chart of the gelcasting process.

A B S T R A C T
Short /uniFB01 bre reinforced ceramic composites, based on ZrB2 with a carbon /uniFB01 bre content of 45 vol%, were produced by ball-milling and rapid Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) at 1800 -2300 °C by varying the holding times. Careful control of the sintering parameters, such as the heating rate, dwell temperature and holding time are required to produce a fully dense matrix and avoid /uniFB01 bre degradation. The sintered materials were characterized in terms of relative density, morphology of the /uniFB01 bres and microstructural features of the matrix. The /uniFB01 nal residual porosity ranged from 16 to 3 vol%. The carbon /uniFB01 bres maintained their original morphology and the matrix consisted of ZrB2, SiC and ZrC phases. In spite of the good adhesion between the /uniFB01 bre surface and the matrix promoted by SPS processing, extensive /uniFB01 bre pull-out was observed in the fracture surfaces of all of the composites sintered at or below 1900 °C.

3.1. Densification behaviour
High-density samples ( > 97 %, see Table 2) were obtained despite a dwell time lower than that reported in literature [48,49]. TW2 samples showed the lowest onset temperature (TON) of 1010 ◦ C. This temperature is quite low compared to other similar TiB2-based materials [50], and is in agreement with the previous work [39]. However, the sintering phenomena start to slow down before the maximum temperature is reached, as indicated by the peak of the densification rate curve occurring well before the dwell step and the plateauing of the curve at 97 % before the start of dwell time (Fig. 1a). This sintering behaviour can be attributed to the presence of Co, which promotes liquid sintering mechanisms that require lower temperatures to be activated, but were not sufficient to complete densification [51]. ZW6 samples reached full densification quickly, while HW6 samples required a longer isothermal hold due to the refractory nature of HfB2. In particular, ZrB2-based material showed the highest densification rate value of 7.3 % min GLYPH<0> 1 allowing full densification within a limited dwell time. It is worth noting that the observed TON of 1400 ◦ C is lower than those reported for ZrB2 with other ceramic sintering aids (Si3N4, SiC, Y2O3, Al2O3, ZrN) [52 -54]. A lower TON was only obtained when using Ni as the sintering aid [55]. However, Ni prevented grain growth, which is desired to increase the thermal conductivity. For this reason, although the activation of densification mechanisms at low temperature and reaching the plateau took less than 15 min, the isothermal hold was extended to 30 min to ensure a coarse and homogeneous microstructure and to raise the thermal conductivity. Noteworthy, the manufacturing process of this work allowed to reduce the starting densification temperature (TON) compared to similar ZrB2 materials [56] containing higher amount of starting WC (15 vol% instead of 4.1 vol%).

2. Materials and methods
Dispersed granular inclusions were obtained by the  method described  in  Ref.  [26].  TaB2 -SiC  powder  mixtures  were  coated  with oleic acid in a rotating ball mill for 14 h. After that, a 1% solution of polyvinyl alcohol was sprayed into an open pot rotating at an angle to the horizon at a speed of 15 rpm using an argon spray gun. The sprayed polyvinyl alcohol solution coated the particles in the mixture and stuck them together. The rotation of the pot led to an increase in the average size of the formed granules. Ceramic granules were sifted through laboratory sieves between mesh 270 and mesh 100, the size of the selected granules ranged from 50 to 150 μ m. The sintering of the granules was carried  out  to  give  them  sufficient  strength  for  further  work  on  the formation of matrix -granule powder mixtures. The sintering temperature was 1600 ◦ C without holding. During the sintering process, an intermediate  holding  for  an  hour  at  300 ◦ C  was  made  to  remove  the organic binder, achieved at a rate of 50 ◦ C h GLYPH<0> 1 . Heating from 300 to 1600 ◦ C was carried out at a rate of 200 ◦ C h GLYPH<0> 1 .
After sintering, the granules had an irregular shape with an average size of 167.3 ± 11.5 μ m, estimated according to the SEM micrograph intercept procedure described in ASTM E112, Fig. 1. A similar approach was applied to estimate the average grain size of sintered ceramics.
The matrix phase and the granules were mixed in a magnetic mixer in an ethanol medium. The final sintering of the studied materials was carried  out  under  a  pressure  of  about  150  MPa  at  a  temperature  of 1800 ◦ C in a vacuum with holding for 20 min. Fig. 2 illustrates the structure  of  the  formed  composites.  The  sample  compositions  are presented in Table 1.
Fig. 1. A general view of TaB2 -SiC granules.

3.5. Machine learning model interpretation
The peak sintering temperature also exerts a positive influence on final material hardness. Extensive studies [39] confirm that densification  behavior  and  grain  growth  in  ceramic  materials  predominantly depend on sintering temperature, holding time, and heating rate. Optimized sintering temperatures enable the fabrication of dense HfB2 materials with refined grains. While relative density increases with elevated sintering temperatures, excessive temperature gains provide diminishing returns on densification and conversely promote grain coarsening, ultimately  reducing  material  hardness  [40].  The  optimal  hardness sample in this study was achieved without employing maximum sintering temperature or holding duration, validating this conclusion and providing guidance for energy-efficient parameter optimization in highhardness UHTC fabrication.
Current  reported  hardness  values  for  HfB2-SiC-X  system  UHTCs predominantly range between 15 -30 GPa, though discrepancies exist due to variations in preparation and testing methodologies. This study successfully identified higher hardness formulations through machine learning-assisted optimization with merely two iterative cycles (8 total samples),  achieving  a  peak  hardness  of  25.13  GPa.  Compared  with conventional trial-and-error approaches, this machine learningintegrated  methodology  demonstrates  superior  efficiency  in  resource conservation while accelerating research progress, offering promising technical support for rapid performance optimization and discovery of novel UHTCs.

2.1. Material manufacturing
All powders were shaped in a disk of 45 mm of diameter under linear pressure of 60 bar. Then the disks were hot-pressed in the vacuum range of 0.1 -1 mbar using an induction-heated graphite die, which allowed a heating rate above 1200 ◦ C/h (the time required for the heating stage from 950 ◦ C was about 25 min), and applying a constant uniaxial pressure of 35 MPa. The sintering temperatures and dwell times are detailed in Table 2. Dwell times were adjusted from 5 to 50 min to achieve a stable shrinkage plateau and a good densification. The change in thickness of the green pellets under hot-pressing was recorded during heating up and final dwell and then converted into relative density data, under the hypothesis of a full mass conservation. The temperature was monitored by means of an optical pyrometer focused into a blind hole dug on the external surface of the graphite die. At the end of the dwell, free cooling followed. The detail of heating rate and the calculated continuous densification of the pellets are showed in Fig. 1.
From the sintered pellets 12.5 mm × 12.5 mm x 1 mm tiles were machined. A 12.5 mm × 12.5 mm surface of the tiles was manually polished using diamond paste from 30 μ m down to 0.25 μ m grain size.

3.2. Microwave sintering
Microwave  sintering  utilizes  the  dielectric  properties  of  ceramic materials within a microwave electromagnetic field to achieve sintering and densification by directly heating the material to the required temperature.  Unlike  traditional  sintering,  which  relies  on  heat  radiation from the surface inward, microwave sintering heats the entire material uniformly by converting absorbed microwave energy into the kinetic and potential energy of the material ' s internal molecules. This method enables rapid heating and sintering, increasing the kinetic energy and diffusion  coefficient  of  molecules  or  ions,  thus  allowing  for  lowtemperature, fast sintering without significant grain growth [78]. The sintering temperature in microwave sintering is typically 100 -300 ◦ C lower than in conventional methods, with a significantly shorter holding time (0 -20 min) and a reduced sintering cycle by 50 % GLYPH<0> 90 %. The process of microwave sintering has several advantages: it eliminates the need for heat conduction, reduces thermal inertia, and allows for instant heating and cooling, making it highly efficient and energy-saving. The resulting material has finer grains and improved mechanical properties, making  microwave  sintering  a  promising  technique  for  producing high-strength, high-toughness, and high-hardness ceramic tools [78].
