1. Introduction
ZrC, a key ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC), has been widely recognized for its capability to provide substantial thermal protection for aerospace vehicles operating in extreme environments [1,2]. However, its performance is hindered by the extremely high melting point (T m ≈ 2710 ◦ C [3]) and detrimental phase-transformation stress [4] of ZrO2, resulting in pulverised oxidation [5,6] and limiting its ability to inhibit  oxygen  diffusion.  Doping  with  Si-based  ceramics  [7,8] forms Si -O glass that can heal surface defects; however, SiO2  evaporates at 1650 ◦ C [9] and SiO sublimates at 1727 ◦ C [10], leading to a porous oxide film. Rare-earth oxides such as La2O3 [11], Yb2O3 [12], and Y2O3 [13] can  stabilise  tetragonal  ZrO2  (t-ZrO2),  thereby  mitigating  stress mismatches; however, these materials also increase the concentration of oxygen vacancies. High-T m components, including TiC [14], W [15], and TaC [16], help maintain the high liquidus temperature of ZrC; however, the thermal stabilities of their oxides are insufficient [17 -19], resulting in discontinuous oxide films. To date, no public reports have demonstrated the formation of dense single-phase ZrO2 films on the surface of ZrC coatings after ablation.
NbC oxidises to a liquid state at 1500 ◦ C in air [20] and forms a dense filmy structure at 2000 -2100 ◦ C under an oxyacetylene flame [21], demonstrating  excellent  wide-temperature  range  flowability.  When

3 High-entropy UHTC for performance optimization
Apart from metal diborides, the performance of high-entropy metal carbides has also been investigated.[93, 94] Wang et al . [88] prepared a high-entropy (TiZrNbTaMo)C ceramic by using a two-step hot pressing method with a hardness value of 25.3 GPa at 9.8 N, which was higher than that of ordinary ultra-high temperature ceramics. The lattice  distortion  caused  by  the  difference  of  atomic  size  leads  to  the  increase  of hardness, which is consistent with the principle of high entropy borides. Apart from the mechanical properties, the oxidation resistance behavior of high-entropy metal carbides is an important aspect as thermal protective coatings for space applications, especially considering the relatively poor oxidation resistance behaviors of metal carbides UHTCs comparing to metal diborides. Recently, Li et al . [95] systematically investigated the ablation  resistance  of  high-entropy  carbides,  including  (Hf-Ta)C,  (Hf-Ta-Nb)C,  and (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C, prepared via pressurelessly sintering at 2100 ℃ for 2h. After 30 s of ablation at 2.4 MW/m² oxyacetylene flame, the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C exhibited the thinnest oxide layer, only 51.5% of (Hf-Ta)C. This performance enhancement is attributed to the enrichment of Ta in HfO2/ZrO2 lattices, which forms low-melting-point phases that compromise the stability of the oxide layer, while the mutual dissolution of Zr and HfO2 forms a (Hf, Zr)O2 solid solution, effectively delaying the structural destabilization of the oxide layer. 3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
Fig.  7d-e  show  the  phase  diagram  of  HfO2-ZrO2  and  ZrO2-Nb2O5 systems. In the HfO2-ZrO2  phase diagram, the lowest liquidus temperature is about 2680 ◦ C. When turning to ZrO2-Nb2O5 binary phase diagram, liquid phase regions (shaded area) are significantly enlarged at similar temperatures, accompanied with the replacement process of lowmelting-point Zr6Nb2O17  for ZrO2, indicating a significant increase in liquid content and instability for both Nb50 and Nb20 coating. In this work, the composition change and corresponding ablation resistance of Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb-O films were highly related to Ta/Nb addition amount and (Hf, Zr)O2-based solid solution skeletons with high-temperature stability. Taking Zr-Nb-O binary system as an example, as discussed in Fig. 7e, the composition range around 2000 ◦ C was in t-ZrO2  or t-ZrO2 + L regions  despite  the  Nb  addition  amount  up  to  40 mol%.  As  samples ' temperature drops to room temperature from the ablation temperature (~2000 ◦ C), the phase precipitation process will occur in different (Hf- Zr-Ta-Nb)C coatings, corresponding to the detected A6B2O17 (A = Hf, Zr; B = Ta, Nb) phases in the XRD patterns (Fig. S4). As for other Hf-Ta-O, Hf-Nb-O, Zr-Ta-O binary systems, similar analytical results were also expected. However, despite that the A6B2O17 phases precipitated in the oxide films when samples cooled down to room temperature, the t-ZrO2 or  t-HfO2 solid  solutions  still  mainly  contributed  to  maintaining  the structure stability of the oxide film under ablation conditions, according to the reported phase diagrams in Fig. 7e.

3.4. Compressive strength and thermal conductivity
This is because high entropy effect can efficiently reduce thermal conductivity of solid skeleton due to significant phonon scattered at the disordered cation sublattice.
Besides, Fig. 6 (b) plots the trends of thermal conductivity of porous samples with different porosities as a function of testing temperature. As can be seen, thermal conductivity of porous high entropy carbide slowly increases with elevated temperatures. Thermal conductivity of porous (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C with porosity of 86.4% is 0.239 W/(m /22c5 K) and 0.436 W/(m /22c5 K) at room temperature and 300 ° C, respectively. Moreover, the sample with higher porosity has lower thermal conductivity than those samples with lower porosity at both room and high temperatures. The values range from 0.436 W/(m /22c5 K) to 0.326 W/(m /22c5 K) when porosities increase from 86.4% to 95.9% at 300 ° C. Specially, thermal conductivity for porous ZrC [34] with porosity of 85% is shown in Fig. 6 (b) for comparison. From room temperature to 300 ° C, thermal conductivity of ZrC has a larger variation from 0.94 W/(m /22c5 K) to 1.35 W/(m /22c5 K), which is much higher than those of porous high entropy carbide with porosity of 86.4% (from 0.239 W/(m /22c5 K) to 0.436 W/(m /22c5 K)). High entropy effect effectively reduces thermal conductivity of UHTC sample. Based on the above comparison, it is clear that our sample has enhanced mechanical and thermal properties than those carbides in previous works, as well as superior properties compared with typical oxides. Hence, it can be concluded that the specimens in this work have better comprehensive performance.

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
To visually compare the effect of varying Nb element content on the surface temperature distribution of coatings, Fig. 7a presents the surface temperature curve and surface temperature field distribution of Nb50, Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings  during  120 s  ablation,  where  the  surface temperatures increased with more Nb content. Fig. 7b shows the average linear ablation rate and average mass ablation rate of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10  coatings  after  120 s  ablation.  The  Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings exhibited thickness increase and weight gain and Nb10 showed smaller change in mass and thickness ( GLYPH<0> 0.65 ± 0.04 μ m/s, GLYPH<0> 0.47 mg/s) change, while Nb50 coating showed significant thickness decline. As reported by Li et al. [19], the service-derived (Hf, Zr)O2 layer of (Hf0.5Zr0.5)C coating under 2.4 MW/m 2 oxyacetylene flame was porous and loose after 120 s ablation, with an average linear ablation rate of GLYPH<0> 0.88 μ m/s, which is also higher than that of Nb10 coating in this work.

Document: Ujah_2025_NextMaterials_Evaluatingtheproperties.pdf. Title: Evaluating the properties and applications of high entropy borides, nitrides and carbides. The header and byline of this document reads: Review article Chika Oliver Ujah * , Peter Apata Olubambi Center for Nanoengineering and Advanced Materials, School of Mining, Metallurgy and Chemical Engineering, University of Johannesburg, South Africa A R T I C L E  I N F O Keywords: High entropy alloys High entropy carbides High entropy ceramics

1. Introduction
Thermal-insulation  materials  capable  of  enduring  ultra-high  temperatures are urgently required by hypersonic vehicle thermal protection systems and nuclear reactor cladding [1 -4]. There are two critical factors  in  developing  ultra-high  temperature  materials  for  thermal insulation:  the  selection  of  an  appropriate  ultra-high  temperature resistance material and the reduction of its thermal conductivity [5 -7]. As a kind of transition metal carbides, Ta4HfC5  is considered as promising  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  because  of  extremely high melting point (4215 K), excellent high-temperature phase stability, and outstanding high-temperature oxidation resistance [8 -10]. The key for the ultra-high temperature thermal insulation application of Ta4HfC5 ceramics is the reduction of the thermal conductivity [11]. Increasing the porosity of the material and regulating its pore structure is one of the main strategies to reduce the thermal conductivity of ceramic materials. The numerous pores with appropriate size and structure in porous ceramics can efficiently hinder solid-phase heat conduction and reduce the thermal conductivity. Additionally, nanoscale ceramic particles can be used to construct the porous ceramic skeleton with nanopores to form hierarchical  pore  structure  and  further  reduce  the  solid-phase  heat conduction and convective heat transfer of Ta4HfC5  ceramic [12 -16].

The application of ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) coatings is  an  effective  way  to  improve  the  in-service  performance  of  CMC structures  at  higher  operating  temperatures  and  for  longer  duration without  dramatically  hampering  the  overall  weight  of  the  structure. Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is the preferred compound, because of its excellent combination of high melting temperature, high thermal conductivity (40 -100 W/m K) [9 -14], and superior ablation [15 -18] and erosion resistance [19,20] . Particularly, ceramics in the ZrB2 - WC - SiC system have shown to possesses outstanding strength retention up to 2100 ◦ C [21] and high thermal erosion resistance at 2600 -2800 ◦ C [22, 23]. As compared to pure ZrB2, these properties seem to be related first to  a  better  oxidation  resistance  of  the  (Zr,W)B2  solid  solution grown around the original ZrB2  cores upon sintering compared to pure ZrB2 [24,25], coupled with the formation of an outermost more protective oxide scale, where possible stabilization of B2O3 glass by W occurs [26], and with the formation of a more dense subscale, where ZrO2  grains encapsulate W solid products, as a consequence of the (Zr,W)B2  solid solution oxidation. 3.1. Ablation behavior of protective (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C system at high temperatures
As  characterized  in  Fig.  2b,  the  sprayed  Ta25Nb25  coating  is composed of carbides, (Hf, Zr)O2 and (Ta, Nb)Ox. The detailed PDF cards for  as-sprayed  coatings  with  different  composition  are  presented  in Fig. S2c. Within the 2 θ range of 30 -40 ◦ , two typical diffraction peaks of carbides were detected, corresponding to the (111) and (200) planes of their  FCC  structures,  while  some  (Ta,  Nb)Ox  oxides  (around  37.5 ◦ ) related  with  Ta  or  Nb  elements  were  also  observed.  Limited  by  the spraying process carried out in an atmospheric environment, the melted and semi-melted carbide powders were inevitably oxidized. In our previous  work  [35],  the  ball  milling  and  agglomeration  (spray  drying technique) process were found to have little effect on the oxidation of original powders. Generally, the maximum temperature during agglomeration process is about 300 ◦ C, lower than the initial oxidation temperatures of carbides. The XRD pattern of the Ta25Nb25 agglomerated powders was supplemented in Fig. S2b. It can be detected that there is no oxide observed in the agglomerated powders, suggesting that the  remaining oxides  in  the  sprayed  coatings  were  generated  by  the thermal spray process.
Fig. 2. Analytical results of Ta25Nb25 agglomerated powder and sprayed coating:(a) surface BSE images of agglomerated powders and sprayed coating; (b) XRD patterns of sprayed coating; (c1-c2) cross-sectional BSE images and corresponding TEM image.

3.2. Mechanism analysis on the addition of low-melting-point components
From another perspective of doping effect of Ta and Nb elements, it was reported [15 -17,34] the critical Ta content for structure instability of  Zr-Ta-O,  Hf-Ta-O  and  Hf-Zr-Ta-O  films  is  no  more  than  20 mol%, while the critical Nb content for structure instability of Zr-Nb-O and Hf-Nb-O films exceeds 30 mol%, suggesting smaller deconstructive effects of Nb addition than that of Ta addition. Table S3 compares the reported  linear  ablation  rates  under  the  same  oxyacetylene  ablation condition (2.4 MW/m 2 , 120 s). The HfC-NbC coating did not exhibit any thickness reduction even with the content of NbC reaching 40 mol%. In contrast, a thickness reduction in HfC-TaC coatings was observed at the TaC  content  of  30 mol%.  As  for  the  ZrC-NbC  coating,  no  thickness reduction was noted with the content of NbC reaching 30 mol%. However, for the ZrC-TaC coating, a thickness reduction occurred at the TaC content of 20 mol%. According to the recorded phenomena in Table S3, the thickness reduction of oxide layer occurred with the addition of TaC, quite different from NbC addition.

A) Thermal properties of high entropy carbides
Generally, thermal conductivity in HECbs is a function of its family and operating temperature, and it maintains relatively high k at elevated temperatures due to significant electronic heat transport, enabling their use in cutting and machining tools. It is imperative to state that the influence  of  configurational  disorder  on  transport  characteristics  is  a function of the dominant heat-carrying mechanism. In HECbs, where electron-mediated  transport  stimulates  significantly  to  total  thermal conductivity, moderate disorder can enhance the electronic density of states and promote carrier mobility at elevated temperature, resulting in higher conductivity. In contrast, thermal transport in HEBs and high entropy monoborides is primarily phonon-driven; here the same disorder raises phonon and mass-fluctuation scattering, suppressing lattice thermal  conductivity.  Thus,  whether  disorder  enhances  or  reduces transport  depends  on  the  balance  between  electronic  and  phononic contributions and the severity of structural distortion.

3.5. Verification on ablation properties of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C systems
Fig. 11a presents the average linear ablation rate and the average mass  ablation  rate  of  Zr70Nb10,  Nb10  and  Zr20Nb10  coatings  after 300 s ablation. Zr20Nb10 coating exhibited significant thinning (0.30 ± 0.15 μ m/s) and weight loss (0.61 mg/s), suggesting severe peeling off under ablation conditions. In contrast, the Zr70Nb10 and Nb10 coatings maintained a thickening trend ( GLYPH<0> 0.09 ± 0.02 μ m/s and GLYPH<0> 0.01 ± 0.07 μ m/s, respectively). Zr70Nb10 coating exhibited weight loss (0.18 mg/ s), while the mass of Nb10 coating continued to increase ( GLYPH<0> 0.04 mg/s), suggesting the best ablation property of Nb10 coating. The comparison of ablation property among different protective coatings reported in the literature [15 -17,19,26,34,52 -55] is displayed in Fig. 11b. The ablation property of reported HfC-ZrC-TaC coating [19] and (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating in  this  work  was  also  presented  in  Table  S5,  both  of  which  showed optimal ablation performance at a Ta/Nb content of around 10 mol%. In particular, the ablation rates of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating was significantly lower than that of HfC-ZrC-TaC coating, despite of longer ablation time. In this work, the as-optimized Nb10 coating (named by (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C  protective  system)  shows  nearly  zero  linear  and mass ablation rate after 300 s ablation protection above 2000 ◦ C, as well as excellent cyclic ablation protection performance.

1. Introduction
Efficient and appropriate composition design of multi-elemental UHTCs for extremely thermal protection is in great demand and promising for carbon-based materials. Based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C protective coating system via plasma spraying, the synergistic effects were clarified through several steps: data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment verification. Results showed that, (Hf0.25Zr0.25Ta0.25Nb0.25)C coating exhibited insufficient protective ability (2000 ◦ C, < 120 s), due to the similar content ratio of high-melting-point and low-melting-point phases. The decline of Ta or Nb relative content effectively improved ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2  skeleton phases. After further screening for the proper composition of Hf, Zr, Ta and Nb elements, the (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C protective system was optimized and verified step by step, which delightfully showed long-term ablation property (2000 ◦ C, > 300 s) with nearly zero linear ablation rate.

question: What were the energy and force prediction errors of the deep potential model trained by Dai et al. 2022 compared to DFT calculations?
answer: The energy and force prediction errors of the deep potential model trained by Dai et al. 2022 compared to DFT calculations are not covered in the provided sources. However, the sources do mention that the prediction errors in energy and force are 15 meV/atom and 400 meV/Å, respectively, which guarantees reliable simulations in this work [1].

2.1. Construction of GBs
A dataset generated by DFT-based calculations was used to train and test the DP model. Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) [36] was adopted to perform DFT-based calculations. Ultrasoft pseudopotential [37] and Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation (PBE-GGA) [38] were applied in the calculations. The cutoff energy of the plane-wave basis set was set to be 500 eV. The k -points mesh according to the Monkhorst-Pack method [39] with a separation of 0.03 ˚ A -1 was adopted in the Brillouin zone. Supercells for non-defective crystals, crystals with vacancies, melts, surfaces, and GBs were constructed. For each supercell, 10 steps of MD simulation under NVT ensemble with time step being 5 fs and temperature randomly chosen from 0 K to 5,0 0 0 K were implemented to generate the DFT dataset. One-tenth of the data were left as a test set, while the others were used to train the DP model. Fig. 2 compares the energies and forces between the DP predictions and the DFT calculations for the test set. Statistics on the prediction errors reveal that the prediction errors in energy and force are 15 meV/atom and 400 meV/ ˚ A, respectively, which guarantees reliable simulations in this work. In addition, the supplemental information (SI) provides more comparisons between the DP model and the DFT-based calculations, which reveal good reliability of the DP model.

II. Calculation Methods
First-principles calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) were performed using the Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) code 19 in order to elucidate the electronic structure, chemical bonding and mechanical properties of NiB. The Vanderbilt-type ultrasoft pseudopotential 20 and generalized gradient approximation based on the Wu-Cohen (WC) scheme 21 for the exchange-correlation functional were used for DFT calculations. Upon calculation, 'spin-polarized' function was checked so that the calculation was performed using different wave functions for different spins. The plane-wave basis set cutoff was 500 eV. The special points sampling integration over the Brillourin zone was employed by using the Monkhorst Pack method with 9 9 3 9 8 special k-points mesh. 22 To minimize the enthalpy and interatomic forces, lattice parameters were modified. In geometry optimization, the Broyden -Fletcher -Goldfarb - Shanno minimization scheme 23 was used. The tolerances for geometry optimization are difference on total energy within 5 9 10 /C0 6 eV/atom, maximum ionic Hellmann -Feynman force within 0.01 eV/ /C23 A, and maximum ionic displacement within 5 9 10 /C0 4 /C23 A and maximum stress within 0.02 GPa. The calculations of projected density of states (PDOS) were performed using a projection of the plane-wave electronic states onto a localized linear combination of atomic orbitals basis set.

2.2. Computational methods
The first-principles calculations were performed within the framework of density functional theory (DFT) using the Cambridge Sequential Total  Energy  Package  (CASTEP)  code  [23].  The  interaction  between valence electrons and ion nuclei was denoted by ultrasoft pseudo-potential [24]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof represented exchange-correlation energy (PBE) scheme within generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [25]. To ensure convergence of the total energy in the ground state, the plane-wave cutoff energy was set to 440 eV. The integration  in  the  Brillouin  zone  (BZ)  was  done  on  a  set  of  special k-points  determined  according  to  the  Monkhorst-Pack  scheme,  the k-points grid of NiB, Ni2B, Ni3B and Ni4B3  are 5 × 2 × 5, 3 × 3 × 3, 3 × 2 × 3 and 1 × 5 × 2 respectively. Geometry optimization was carried out using the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) method, which minimizes the energy and enhances stability by optimizing the system parameters  [26].  During  the  optimization  process,  the  total  energy convergence threshold was set to 5.0 × 10 ⁻⁶ eV/atom, the force on each atom was kept below 0.01 eV/Å, the maximum atomic displacement was within 5.0 × 10 ⁻⁴ Å, and the maximum stress was below 0.02 GPa. After geometry optimization, the elastic constants (Cij) of the Ni-B material were determined based on the stress-strain relationship and fitted linearly using Hooke ' s law [27]. The results are shown in Table S1.

2.3. DFT calculations
First-principles  calculations  were  performed  with  the  Vienna  Abinitio Simulation Package ( VASP ) code based on the density functional theory (DFT). The exchange-correlation effects were processed by the generalized  gradient  approximation  (GGA)  with  the  Perdew-BurkeErnzerhof (PBE) function [22 -24]. Interactions between ionic real and valence electrons were determined by applying the Projector-Augmented Wave (PAW) pseudopotential. The valence configuration are 5 d 2 6 s 2 ,  5 d 3 6 s 2 and 2 s 2 2 p 2 for Hf, Ta and C, respectively. After the convergence test, the plane wave cut-off energy was set to 600 eV, and the matching Monkhorst GLYPH<0> Pack k-points grid sampling was set to be 15 × 15 × 15 for the Brillouin zone [25]. After the full optimization of the cell, the total energies were converged well to 5 . 0 × 10 GLYPH<0> 6  eV/atom, the maximum ionic Hellmann-Feynman forces convergence value was no more than 0.01 eV/Å, and the maximum of the stress tensor was less than 0.02 GPa.

The results indicate that the di/uniFB00usion of Zr in the YSi-O system exhibits relative higher di/uniFB00usion barriers (2.53 eV), comparing to that in the SiO 2 system (1.77 eV). Therefore, the t -Zr0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 grains are isolated by Y-Si-O phase in agglomerated particles ( Figure 4e,f), and the growth of t -Zr 0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 crystals www.advancedsciencenews.com www.advancedscience.com is restricted due to the high di/uniFB00usion energy of Zr in the Y-Si-O system and stronger interaction of Y-Si-O -t -Zr 0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 . Overall, the results of DFT calculations provide the guidance of the structural design for oxides layer, which is of significance for the further studies on reusable UHTCMCs.

2.1. Construction of GBs
The machine learning potential proposed by Zhang et al. [ 32 , 33 ] is adopted to train an interatomic potential for multi-element transition metal carbides from a dataset generated by DFT calculations. The model is called DP, which maps the local atomic configurations to atomic energies by a deep neural network [ 32 , 33 ], and has been proven to be applicable to high entropy materials [ 34 , 35 ]. The DP model is comprised of two sets of neural networks. The first one is a descriptor net, which automatically encodes local atomic configurations to symmetry-preserving descriptors. The second one is a fitting net, which maps the symmetry-preserving descriptors to atomic energies. In this work, the architecture of DP was set as follows: (1) the descriptor was a smooth edition descriptor from reference [32] , which consisted of 3 layers of residual net with each layer having 40, 80, and 160 nodes; (2) the cutoff was set to be 6.5 ˚ A with a smooth function imposed from 6.0 ˚ A; (3) the projection dimension of the descriptor was set to be 16; (4) the 'type-one' technology was adopted to reduce the scale of the descriptor net; (5) the fitting net included 4 layers with each layer having 160, 160, 160, and 160 nodes. For details of the DP parameters, please refer to the original papers and the open-source software [ 32 , 33 ].

3.1. Electronic structure and dielectric functions
The  electronic  structures  of  transition  metal  diborides  have  been extensively  investigated  previously  by  DFT  calculations  and  experiments [16,17,28,29]. Generally accepted features are the finite density of states and a pseudogap at Fermi level, indicating these systems are metallic and strongly-bonded [16]. The bonding states below the Fermi level are dominated by the TM to TM d -d interactions and B to B p -p interactions, while the anti-bonding states are mainly from TM d orbitals [16]. Due to the similarity in electronic structure, only the band structure of ZrB2  is illustrated for simplicity. Since the energy of solar spectrum is not higher than 5 eV, the bands from DFT calculations (solid lines) in energy range from GLYPH<0> 15 eV to 10 eV and QP corrected bands (blue  and  red  dash  lines)  in  energy  range  from GLYPH<0> 10 eV to 5 eV are presented in Fig. 2(a). With QP correction considered, the metallic nature of ZrB2 is preserved, the main difference between original DFT band structure and corrected one is the broadening of energy gap where no crossing of valence and conductive bands is found. Since the interband dielectric  function  is  directly  determined  by  the  band  structure,  QP correction is vital to the reliable prediction of optical properties.

3.2. Modeling
A grid search was conducted on the training set to determine the optimal  set  of  hyperparameters  for  each  model,  and  the  hyperparameters are listed in Table 4. The mean absolute error ( MAE ) and mean squared error ( MSE ) of the model predictions on the test set reveal that among the 8 models (Fig. 7), the Random forest model exhibits the highest prediction accuracy with an mean absolute error ( MAE ) of 2.75 μ m s GLYPH<0> 1 and an mean squared error ( MSE ) of 7.3 ( μ m s GLYPH<0> 1 ) 2 , whereas the performance  of  the  other  models  on  this  dataset  is  suboptimal.  The scatter  plots  of  the  model ' s  training  and  prediction  data  also  clearly demonstrate that the Random forest model ' s data points are closest to the 45-degree diagonal line for both the training and test sets (Fig. 8), with the coefficient of determination ( R 2 ) for training and prediction reaching 0.81 and 0.71, respectively, indicating relatively high predictive precision. Consequently, we select the Random forest model as the surrogate model for subsequent analysis of the ablation resistance of UHTCMCs.
The  Shapley  additive  explanations  (SHAP)  values  based  on  the Random forest model are shown in Fig. 8. Negative Shapley additive explanations  values  indicate  that  a  feature  negatively  influences  the J. Xiao et al.
Fig. 5. Feature importance assessment using 6 tree models.
Fig. 6. Ranking chart of feature importance based on evaluation weight of 6 tree models.

2.1. Construction of GBs
Simulating GBs of medium/high entropy ceramics is challenging due to the chemical complexity of the random solid solu- tion state. Though density functional theory (DFT) based methods can in principle deal with the chemical complexity well, their computational demanding nature impedes their application to GBs of medium/high entropy materials, where thousands of atoms or more are necessary to model the chemical complexity. Thanks to the advancement in artificial intelligence, a new paradigm, machine learning potential, has come forth in recent years [29-32] . The machine learning potential bridges the gap between DFT-based methods and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, which guarantees atomistic simulations with accuracy comparable to DFT-based methods at a cost comparable to MD simulations.

The green line illustrates the trace of the grain boundary, and the green points are coincident sites in the boundary. The box enclosed by the dash lines and the green line is the primitive cell of the CSL, which is 5 times that of the primitive cell of the crystal (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

A B S T R A C T
In this study, we used the ANN to achieve the aim to predict melting points of non-oxide UHTCs. In order to improve the accuracy of the model, we /uniFB01 rst made a data cleaning of the dataset, forming a huge dataset containing most of non-oxide UHTCs. Then, we adjusted the accuracy of model prediction by training and testing the model, and analyzed the in /uniFB02 uence of network structure in accuracy, while several reported data have been used to ensure the accuracy of the model. We set Hf-C-N system as an example to introduce the ability of the prediction model, whose average relative error is as low as 5%. Finally, we gave a theoretical interpretation by using of the materials thermodynamics and the density functional theory (DFT). The novel material developing process is successfully applied in the melting points

predication of non-oxide UHTCs by taking the advantage of the highperformance computing and theoretical calculation.

3.2. Machine learning model prediction and selection
The predictive accuracy of ML models is highly dependent on the quantity and quality of datasets. Given the limited size of the original dataset (83 entries), we aimed to enhance model accuracy through data augmentation,  thereby  enabling  the  trained  surrogate  model  to  efficiently guide active learning for rapid discovery of high-performance compositions. To align with the active learning framework, the reciprocal of oxide layer thickness was adopted as the output target during model  training  and  prediction,  facilitating iterative optimization. Twelve  ML  models  were  implemented  using  Python ' s  scikit-learn  library. The original dataset (83 entries) and the combined dataset (1083 entries,  original  and  augmented)  were  separately  used  for  model training. The oxide layer thickness in the combined dataset was mathematically transformed by taking its reciprocal to serve as the prediction target. Both datasets were normalized and randomly split into training (80 %) and testing (20 %) subsets for independent model training and evaluation. Model performance was assessed using the coefficient of R², MAE and MSE on the test set. To ensure robustness, all models underwent  100  randomized  training  and  prediction  cycles,  with  reported metrics representing averages across these iterations.

To further enhance the predictive fidelity of oxide scale growth under service-relevant conditions, future research could focus on integrating advanced Finite Element Modeling (FEM) approaches with multilayer diffusion frameworks. This would improve the model's ability to simulate the complicated interplay between transport kinetics and mechanical constraints, encompassing oxide scale spallation which may arise from stress accumulation and/or grain boundary diffusion.

2.3. Machine learning model construction and training
Twelve classical ML models were employed: Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Regression (SVR), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), Gradient Boosted Trees (GBT), LightGBM, Ridge Regression, AdaBoost, CatBoost, XGBoost, k-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Decision Tree Regression (DTR), and Gaussian Process Regression (GPR). Hyperparameters were optimized via randomized search prior to training. The dataset was split into training (80 %) and testing (20 %) subsets, with the former used for model training and the latter for performance evaluation. Tenfold cross-validation was applied, and model performance was assessed using the coefficient of determination (R²), mean squared error (MSE), and MAE. R 2 is a statistical factor. The closer it is to 1, the higher the degree of closeness between the data and the fitting line. The latter two indicators reflect the gap between the predicted values and the actual values of the model. The closer the error is to 0, the higher the prediction accuracy of the model.

2.6. DFT calculation
The binding energies [36] of (Hf, Zr)O2 solid solutions (space group: Fm-3m) with different Hf/Zr molar ratios were calculated to evaluate the structural stability, utilizing CASTEP (Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package). The detailed parameters used in DFT calculation were listed in the following: (i) cut-off energy: 400 eV; (ii) k-point: custom grid parameters;  (iii)  tolerance: < 1 × 10 GLYPH<0> 5 eV/atom;  (iv)  maximum  ionic force: < 0.03 eV/Å;  (v)  maximum  stress: < 0.05 GPa;  (vi)  maximum ionic displacement: < 0.001 Å.

question: According to Zapata-Solvas 2018, what phase was detected on the external surface of HS20 samples at temperatures higher than 1800°C during compressive deformation?
answer: According to Zapata-Solvas 2018, the phase detected on the external surface of HS20 samples at temperatures higher than 1800°C during compressive deformation is HfB. [1]

chunks.text:
3.2. HS20 microstructural characterization after deformation
The concept of SiC-reinforced MeB2 comes from the exceptional strength shown by SiC-ZrB2 composites, as high as ∼ 1 GPa [18], and the increased fracture toughness of MeB2 with SiC additions, from values of ∼ 3 for monolithic MeB2 to values as high as ∼ 7 MPa/m 1/2 for composites with SiC [19]. However, this trend reverses at high temperature as SiC acts as a softening agent. A possible reason for this softening is the formation of an eutectic in the Me-B-C ternary system with a melting point of 2390 °C for Zr-B-C, which is nearly 1000 °C lower than the melting temperature of ZrB2 [47]. In addition, the Me-BC phase diagrams reported by Rudy and Windisch [48] suggestthat the combination of MeC + MeB2 might be of interest at temperatures lower than 1500 °C as there could be temperature-dependent B-C exchange reactions associated with volume changes in the carbide particles, resulting in bodies with poor mechanical properties and subsequently limiting their high temperature potential use at temperatures above 1500 °C. If we consider the results observed here and previous available data [26,39,40,43], including the e /uniFB00 ect of 1 wt.% B in SiC, it could be concluded that B also has a negative e /uniFB00 ect on the high temperature mechanical behavior of MeB2 + SiC systems. Fig. 4, shows XRD patterns of HS20 external surfaces before and after deformation revealing that at temperatures higher than 1800 °C, the formation of HfB is detected at the surface. However, XRD from an internal surface in the bulk does not show formation of HfB at any temperature. Plastic deformation is a phenomenon controlled by the bulk, so this observation could be considered as a con /uniFB01 rmation of the existence of a B /uniFB02 ux from the surface towards the bulk.

3.3. HS20La high temperature mechanical properties
La2O3 addition does not act as a high temperature structural reinforcement. In addition, powders were observed immediately around the sample after testing at 1800 °C, a smaller amount (< 10 mg) of powder was observed at 1900 °C but no trace of powder was observed at 2000 °C, which could be related to the development of either porosity next to the external surface or a chemical reaction during deformation. C content of the powders found at 1800 °C was 1.70 ± 0.02 wt.%. However, the amounts of powder collected at 1900 °C were not enough for C content analysis. Considering that C content for HS20La is 2.01 wt.%, the latter /uniFB01 nding suggests that SiC might segregate in Si and free C at 1800 °C. Moreover, the higher the temperature, the lower the porosity detected at the external surface as di /uniFB00 usion phenomena are more active balancing the presence of any damaging mechanisms such as cavitation or reaction (Fig. 9). For example, Fig. 9a shows the cross section after compression testing at 1800 °C, in which the highest degree of porosity is observed and is reduced as temperature increases to 1900 °C (Fig. 9b) and 2000 °C (Fig. 9c). In a previous study, La2O3 particles were observed to be located next to SiC particles [19] which could have an in /uniFB02 uence on the SiC disociation as this phenomena was not observed in HS20. However, after deformation at 2000 °C there was no O in what were originally La2O3 particles as shown in Fig. 10. The remnant La2O3 particle contains La-Si-Hf-B after deformation, which suggests that O might react with C from SiC particles. Also, the stoichiometry of the SiBn particles is di /uniFB00 erent in HS20 than HS20La: the maximum n in HS20 is 2 whereas it is 12 for HS20La.

3.2. HS20 microstructural characterization after deformation
Furthermore, an increase in the SiC cluster size was observed when testing at or above 1800 °C through post mortem SEM only [23]. Therefore, it is important that future studies give improved microstructural characterization to enable a better understanding of the phenomena involved in high temperature deformation.
Melendez-Martinez et al. [44] showed; (i) ZrB2 at 1500 °C is 200 times more creep resistant than HS30 and (ii) the deformation mechanism is grain boundary sliding and that the deformation of individual ZrB2 grains could be neglected as well as any contribution to deformation from ZrB2-ZrB2 grain boundaries. Therefore, grain boundaries of secondary phases with ZrB2 and with the secondary phases are responsible for the macroscopic deformation and link the B di /uniFB00 usion to form new secondary phases in the Hf-B-Si and Hf-B-Si-C systems as the existing accommodation process of the deformation mechanism. However, it is di /uniFB03 cult to assess how the formation of these secondary phases start and how each phase contributes to macroscopic deformation. Future studies at low deformations will try to answer how instabilities are triggered and how deformation is controlled in the initial stage of plastic deformation. In summary, our study suggests that HS20 behaves at high temperature as follows; (i) a power law creep with thermal activation process accommodated by B lattice di /uniFB00 usion (n=3 and Q=242 kJ/mol) in the temperature range 1500 -1800 °C, consistent with the observation of dislocations by TEM, (ii) grain boundary sliding/pure di /uniFB00 usional mechanism accommodated by B grain boundary di /uniFB00 usion (n=1 and Q=334 kJ/mol), consistent with the absence of dislocations and the generation of new phases in the HfB-Si and Hf-B-Si-C systems.

3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
2b shows activation energy versus SiC content calculated by Talmy et al. for ZrB2 in which the activation data for this study are included. Note that Talmy et al. ' s study is from 1200 to 1500 °C. Therefore, the activation energy used to make the comparison is the one obtained in this study in the lower temperature range studied (1500 -1800 °C). Good agreement between our estimated activation energy and the activation energies of Talmy et al. [27] is shown in Fig. 2b, suggesting that SiC content might have a stronger in /uniFB02 uence on the plastic response of MeB2 (Me = Zr, Hf) UHTCs than the kind of MeB2 analyzed. This statement is supported by the plastic response of 1 wt.% boron-doped SiC in Fig. 2b at 1758 °C, which is similar to the plastic response of HS20 at 1800 °C, more creep resistant than ZS30 at 1800 °C, and shows a strong in /uniFB02 uence of B in the high temperature behavior of SiC. Liquid-phase sintered SiC and additive-free nano β -SiC are more than 3 orders of magnitude more creep resistant than 1 wt.% B-doped SiC at the same temperature ( ∼ 1750 °C) [39,40,43], highlighting the enormous enhancement of creep rate or di /uniFB00 usion rate during plastic deformation by an addition of 1 wt.% B. There is a lack of data about plasticity of monolithic MeB2 to con /uniFB01 rm the role of SiC, but Melendez-Martinez et al. studied the plastic response under compressive creep of ZrB2 from 1400 to 1600 °C [44]. In addition, it was reported that at 1500 °C ZrB2 deforms at ∼ 10 -7 s -1 under 400 MPa, which is ∼ 300 times less resistant than HS20 reported herein in spite of ZrB2 having a porosity of 13.5 vol.% [44].

3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
Concerning the high temperature creep properties of MeB2 reinforced with MeC, there are a couple of studies in /uniFB02 exure for ZrB2-20 vol.% ZrC and TiB2-20 vol.% TiC in addition to other compositions with MeC contents in excess of 40 vol.%, which are far from the interest of this study [45,46]. ZrB2 + 20 vol.% ZrC deforms at 2000 °C in the stress range 5 -30 MPa with a strain rate range of 1.3-8.7 × 10 -3 s -1 respectively and a stress exponent of n=1, suggesting grain boundary sliding as the deformation mechanism. However, the ZrB2-20 vol.% ZrC is nearly 100 -150 times less creep resistant than the ZrB2-20 vol.% ZrC studied by Gangireddy et al. [26] and HS20 from this study. In addition, contamination during milling of up to 6 wt.% with WC-Co was reported, which could explain the poor creep response of ZrB2-20vol.% ZrC studied by Kats et al. [45] as Co melts at 1500 °C. This example illustrates how important it is to minimize contamination as undesired additional second phases can have a detrimental e /uniFB00 ect on physical properties at temperatures of 2000 °C or higher. Furthermore, monolithic ZrB2 and TiB2 were also studied at temperatures higher than 2000 °C [45,46]. However, if an extrapolation is made to 2000 °C, they are 100 times less creep resistant than ZS30 in the best case, which highlights the importance of controlling second phase impurities as well as the need to obtain reliable data on plasticity of monolithic MeB2.
Fig. 1. Stress versus strain for HS20 at; a) 1500 °C, b) 1800 °C, c) 1900 °C and d) 2000 °C. Strain rates ( ε ˙ ) for each deformation state and subsequent stress exponents (n) are shown.
Fig. 3. Comparison of the strain rate versus stress from HS20 with previously reported data.

3.3. HS20La high temperature mechanical properties
Assuming the conditions of temperature and stresses described above, 1800 °C and 400 MPa, and the results from this study (Fig. 8), we suggest (i) HS20 is deforming at 3 × 10 -4 s -1 and (ii) HS20La at 10 -3 s -1 . In addition, plastic deformation at 1800 °C starts at around 4% and 3% for HS20 and HS20La (Fig. 1 and 8), so that at stresses below 400 MPa there will be no dimension changes for 130 s and 30 s respectively. Moreover, 10% deformation will be reached in 330 s and 100 s respectively. In a sharp leading edge component the deformation should not go above 10% under any circumstances due to the negative impact on /uniFB02 ight aerodynamics. Therefore, considering that an atmospheric re-entry lasts around 10 min being at least half of the time under the hardest conditions, i.e. 1800C and 400 MPa in this discussion, only HS20 would be able to withstand it with a deformation below 10% (9% approximately) while HS20La would be far above 20% deformation, mechanically failing before the end of the re-entry. Ideally, the material should not exceed elastic deformation to be reusable from a structural point of view although those materials exceeding the elastic deformation developing a certain shape change such as HS20 might be suitable for single use. Considering other UHTCs studied, ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC deforms at 5 × 10 -4 s -1 , which means that it is slightly out of the secure range de /uniFB01 ned in this study as a 10% deformation and the elastic threshold (4%) will be reached in 200 s and 80s, respectively. ZrB2-20 vol.% SiC samples tested under plasma wind tunneling testing conditions showed the tip of a sharp leading edge was slightly curved and deformed afterwards even if testing times were no longer than 50 s [54]. Note that 1800 °C and 400 MPa are mild re-entry conditions and ideally materials capable of withstanding re-entries with temperatures in excess of 2000 °C are needed in the long term.

3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
Fig. 1 shows the creep response of HS20 from 1500 °C to 2000 °C approximately over the strain rate range 2.8 × 10 -5 s -1 and 1.4 × 10 -4 s -1 for strains as high as 24%. At 1500 °C (Fig. 1a), HS20 shows a limited ductility (< 6%) for strain rates ε ˙ <6×10 -5 s -1 . At higher strain rates, the deformation is increased to nearly 12% and signi /uniFB01 cant softening is also observed. Deformation-induced softening could be related to either cavitation or phase instabilities. In addition, true steady states of deformation are only observed at 1900 °C or higher temperatures as softening is still active at 1800 °C. Stresses for the steady states of deformation were calculated through extrapolation of steady state to the initiation of the plastic regime at 1900 °C and 2000 °C, as plotted in Fig. 2a. Calculated stress exponents for the steady states of plastic deformation are shown in Fig. 2a, ranging from 3 to 1 as the temperature increases from 1500 °C to 2000 °C. In addition, calculated activation energies are 242 and 334 kJ/mol in the temperature ranges 1500 -1800 °C and 1900 -2000 °C respectively, suggesting the activation of di /uniFB00 erent deformation mechanisms as the stress exponent also changes from 3 to 1 in these same temperature ranges. The last deformation state in Fig. 1c and 1d is not a true stationary as some softening is observed. Therefore, the stress exponent for this last deformation state might not be accurate and this state is not considered for the plot of stationary strain rates versus stress, so the stress exponent shown in Fig. 2a is more accurate than the one displayed for the last deformation state in Fig. 1c and 1d respectively. In addition, no true stationaries were observed at 1800C either, but the data points were considered to allow comparison with previously reported data. The satisfactory agreement of the calculated activation energy value with literature data in Fig. 2b suggests that it was a reasonable assumption.

3.2. HS20 microstructural characterization after deformation
Volatilization of B is unlikely as HfB2 and B are stable in low vacuum atmospheres ( ∼ 10 -2 Torr).
Fig. 4. XRD of the external surface of HS20 after deformation at 1800, 1900 and 2000 °C. XRD before deformation looks identical than after deformation at 1800 °C.

3.3. HS20La high temperature mechanical properties
In addition, SiBn particles with n > 2 contain minor amounts of La, indicating that La plays a role in the formation of new SiBn phases during deformation, which are not formed otherwise. SiBn as a solid solution with n as high as 32 has been reported [49]. Also, some of the SiBn decomposes through a peritectic reaction involving liquid formation, e.g. SiB6 (observed in Fig. 10), which decomposes to a liquid plus SiBn with n > 6. The decomposition temperature of SiB6 is 1850 °C but how Hf and La could a /uniFB00 ect the stability of SiB6 is unknown. Our study indicates that if decomposition reaction occurs, it has to be above 1900 °C as a stress exponent of 3 could be a clear indication of dissolution-precipitation deformation mechanism in ceramics where a liquid is present at the grain boundaries. This is also indicated by the presence of rounded grains in Fig. 10 on SiBn particles with n > 6. Disociation/Decomposition of SiC or SiBn phase could be responsible for the mechanical resistance degradation observed in the stress-deformation curves (Fig. 8). However, to clarify which phase is responsible for the mechanical response degradation, the initial stage of deformation should be studied in detail, which is beyond the scope of this work and will be the subject of future studies. Nonetheless, the poor creep resistance shown by HS20La highlights an unsatisfactory behavior for hypersonic re-entry involving temperatures in excess of 1800 °C. Besides, the use of RE-oxides or any oxide is not recommended according to these results in spite of having the ability to either develop dense-oxidation-resistant RE-zirconates/hafnates layers or stabilize oxidation formation through the presence of Zr-O-C/Hf-O-C particles.

A B S T R A C T
The composition that has received most attention to date and is currently considered as a baseline UHTCs composition for hypersonic TPS development is SiC-reinforced UHTCs, with a SiC content ranging from 10 to 30 vol.% [2,9]. Oxidation of SiC produces an outer BS protective glass that could protect UHTCs components up to ∼ 1650 °C, at which temperature pure SiO2 melts and bulk UHTCs would be exposed to continuous oxidation. Although the BS coating melts at lower temperatures it could withstand temperatures up to 2000 °C for up to 1 h under static oxidation conditions [12 -17]. However, this liquid layer will be blown away during a hypersonic reentry.
On the other hand, hypersonic applications require high strength at high temperature and estimated conditions for hypersonic re-entry are ∼ 1800 °C and ∼ 400 MPa [3]. Furthermore, the latter conditions are at the less harsh end of reentry conditions and in the long term, the

question: What was the mass ablation rate of the Ren 2017 ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating with HfC nanowires after 90 seconds of oxyacetylene torch ablation?
answer: The mass ablation rate of the Ren 2017 ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating with HfC nanowires after 90 seconds of oxyacetylene torch ablation was -0.12 mg/s [1][3].

chunks.text:
a b s t r a c t
To improve the ablation resistance of the coated carbon/carbon composites, HfC nanowires acting as reinforcement materials were introduced into ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating. Synthesis, morphology, mechanical properties and ablation resistance of the coating were researched. A porous HfC nanowire layer was uniformly covered on SiC coating. Both of the toughness and interface bonding strength of ZrB2-SiC coating with HfC nanowires were higher than those of the coating without HfC nanowires. HfC nanowires could effectively inhibit the cracking and peeling of outer coating during ablation. After oxyacetylene torch ablation for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of the coated sample without and with HfC nanowires was 0.20 mg/s and -0.12 mg/s, respectively.
© 2017 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
on C/C composites through three-step process including pressing, pyrolysis and reactive silicon in /uniFB01 ltration, and then analyzed the ablation performance of the coating. Previous research proved that ZrB2-SiC multiphase coating could prevent C/C composites from ablation in severe environment. Among all of the preparation methods, SAPS is a very high-ef /uniFB01 ciency and low-cost method to prepare a dense ZrB2-SiC coating [14,15]. The thickness and composition of the coating could be controlled by adjusting the spraying parameters.

3.3. Ablation behavior of the coatings
Ablation performance of the coatings was measured under oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame. After being ablated for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of pure ZrB2-SiC/SiC coated C/C sample is 0.20 mg/s. While, the mass ablation rate of the coated sample with HfC nanowires is positive ( -0.12 mg/s). During ablation, on the one hand, the coating would gain weight because of its oxidation; on the other hand, mechanical denudation and volatilization of oxide layer would lead to weight loss. By incorporating nanowires, the weight gain of the sample is predominant, so the mass ablation rate is minus. Above results imply that HfC nanowires could inhibit the oxidized layer being denudated, which could reduce the mass loss of the oxide layer.
XRDpattern of the coating with HfC nanowires after ablation for 90 s is shown in Fig. 8. After ablation, ZrO2 phase emerges and the ZrB2 and SiC phases disappear. In ablation environment, ZrB2 would be oxidized to ZrO2 and B2O3, and SiC would be oxidized to SiO2, SiO, CO and CO2. After ablation, only ZrO2 remained on the sample surface owing to its high melting point (2953 K). SiO2, SiO, B2O3 and other gaseous products wouldbeevaporated /uniFB01 ercely in ablation environment, so they could not be found in the XRD pattern.

4. Conclusions
HfC nanowires acting as reinforcement materials for ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating were synthesized by CVD. Hardness, elastic modulus and fracture toughness of the outer coating were all improved because of the incorporation of HfC nanowires. After incorporating HfC nanowires, the interface bonding strength between inner and outer coating increased from 5.9 to 7.5 N. During ablation test, HfC nanowires could inhibit the cracking and peeling of outer coating, resulting in an improvement of ablation resistance of the coating. After ablation for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of ZrB2-SiC/SiC coated sample with HfC nanowires decreased from 0.20 to -0.12 mg/s.

3.3.  Ablation test results
The results of ablation test including weight change percentage (ΔW %), mass ablation rate (Rm), thickness change (% ∆l) and linear ablation rate (Rl) after the test for single-layer SiC and double-layer C/SiC/ZrB2 coatings are presented in Table 2. In order to investigate the performance of the coatings at different temperatures, they exposed to oxypropane and oxyacetylene flame separately. Results show that in the oxyacetylene flame test, the ablation rate of samples is higher than the oxypropane flame. It could be attributed to the higher temperature of oxyacetylene  flame  (about  3000  °C)  in  comparison  with  oxypropane flame (about 2200 °C). On the other hand, in both oxyacetylene and oxypropane  tests,  ablation  rate  has  raised  by  increasing  the  testing time. Considering the data in Table 2, the ablation rate of SiC coating under oxypropane flame after 60s is 5.28 × 10 -4 and the ablation rate of the SiC/ZrB2  coating after 300 s is only 0.426 × 10 -4 . This shows that, the double layer SiC/ZrB2 coating has a higher ablation resistance compared with single-layer SiC coating.
Fig. 9. XRD analysis of: (a) unshrouded (b) solid shrouded SiC/ZrB2 coating.
Fig. 11. SEM image and EDS analysis from SiC coating after ablation test.

3.2. Ablation properties of Cf/ZrC -SiC composites in oxyacetylene torch
An  ablation  test  of  Cf/ZrC -SiC  composites  is  conducted  using  a custom built oxyacetylene torch for 60 s and the mass loss and erosion depth are measured to be 0.52 g (mass ablation rate of 8.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 g/s) and  0.66 mm  (linear  ablation  rate  of  11 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 mm/s),  respectively. After oxyacetylene torch test, the ablated surface of Cf/ZrC -SiC composites can be divided into three zones from center to external based on the appearances, i.e. central pit (zone 1 in Fig. 3c), oxidized zone (zone 2 in Fig. 3c) and unaffected zone (zone 3 in Fig. 3c), corresponding well with  the  distribution  of  oxyacetylene  torch  temperature  (inset  in Fig. 3a). As far as the oxyacetylene torch is concerned, it involves high heat flux (~4 MW/m 2 ), high flow velocity ( > 150 m/s), high temperature (~3000 ◦ C), high heating/cooling rates (500 -1000 ◦ C/s) and severe  oxidizing  environment  (high  mole  fractions  of  molecular  and atomic oxygen) [20]. Nevertheless, both the flow velocity and torch temperature decrease sharply away from the flame core, i.e. in the radial directions. In our case, the distance between torch exit and specimen surface was 10 mm. At this location, the flame core has the high flow velocity and temperatures (~3000 ◦ C) and erodes the materials surface like a drill, leaving not only the ablated pit but also the splashing ZrO2 droplet (Fig. 3c). However, the flame has extreme radial gradients of temperature and velocity (Fig. 3a, b). Therefore, the outer oxidized zone experiences lower temperatures (1800 -2200 ◦ C) and much lower flow velocity, so this zone is severely oxidized but not eroded.

5. Conclusions and outlook

question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2014, Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) was used to process ZS20 and ZS20La, but the resulting microstructure showed that La2O3 location affected SiC particle dispersion. How did the proximity of La2O3 to SiC particles influence the homogeneity of the microstructure in the La2O3-doped composition?
answer: The proximity of La2O3 to SiC particles in the La2O3-doped composition (ZS20La) influenced the homogeneity of the microstructure by reducing the formation of SiC agglomerates. La2O3 particles, due to their closer density to SiC than to HfB2, assisted in the dispersion of SiC particles, leading to a more homogeneous distribution of SiC throughout the matrix compared to HfB2-based composites [6]. However, in ZS20La, the presence of La2O3 near SiC particles resulted in a less homogeneous SiC dispersion compared to ZS20, as indicated by the lower fracture toughness of ZS20La compared to ZS20 [3]. This suggests that while La2O3 can improve SiC dispersion in HfB2-based composites, its proximity to SiC in ZrB2-based composites may have a less favorable effect on the homogeneity of the microstructure.

chunks.text:
2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ =6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously. 10 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, as shown in previous work 10 and Fig. 1, are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density); (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ = 10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ = 3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma-Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2, ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2, HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described in a previous work. 22 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, previously described, 22 are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density) except monolithic ZrB2 which has a density ∼ 95% as a consequence of O impurities which prevent its full densification; (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

2. Experimental procedure
HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 μ m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( α -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 μ m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 μ m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously [10]. The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs [11,19] are; (I) high density (> 99% of theoretical relative density); (II) HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 μ m; and (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles and improve the SiC dispersion throughout the HfB2 matrix.

3.1. Microstructure and composition of the as-fabricated composites
The volume fractions of ZrB2, SiC and La2O3 phases is determined as normalized volume fractions excluding open porosity which are illustrated in Fig. 2 a .  Due to the content of La2O3 in the composites is limited, X-Ray diffraction peaks of La2O3 are not detected in Fig. 2 b . Besides, the SiC formed by PCS at 1100 ℃ is  amorphous phase which cannot be detected by XRD in Fig. 2 b . The density and open porosity of the composites were determined and shown in Table 1.
Take sample ZS 4.6 L as an example, the microstructure and element distribution of the composites are characterized by SEM and EDS and illustrated in Fig. 3. The sample ZS 4.6 L shows a similar microstructure feature with ZS which has a multi-layer structure [27,28]. The SI method is efficient to introduce the ZrB2-La2O3  powders into the inter-bundle area of the fiber fabrics. The intra-bundle area is difficult to fill up by ZrB2-La2O3  during SI due to the impeding of the carbon fibers. Therefore,  ZrB2-La2O3 mainly  concentrates  in  the  inter-bundle  area.  By contrast, the SiC matrix can be introduced into the intra-bundle area effectively during the PIP process, due to the PCS precursor is liquid. Therefore, the La2O3 modified Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites present a specific multi-layer structure. Otherwise, the La2O3  covered by SiC phase and distributes in inter-bundle area which prevents the contact and reaction among La2O3, PyC interface and carbon fibers. The PyC interface and fibers remain complete without clear damage.

2. Experimental procedure
Large agglomerates of SiC arise from the density difference between SiC ( ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 ) and HfB2 ( ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 ), which promotes their non-homogeneous mixing during thermo-rotary drying of HfB2-based UHTCs solutions. The use of a surfactant might overcome this limitation and is being investigated. HS20La contains some SiC agglomerates of ∼ 10 𝛍 m, which is about half the size of the HS20 agglomerates. The location of La2O3 ( ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 ) particles near to SiC particles and its closer density to SiC than HfB2 assist particle dispersion in the solutions reducing the formation of SiC agglomerates. However, SiC distribution is more homogeneous throughout ZrB2 matrix than HfB2 matrix due to lower density difference between SiC ( ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 ) and ZrB2 ( ρ =6.085 g/cm 3 ) than between SiC ( ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 ) and HfB2 ( ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 ), which prevents second phase particles agglomeration in UHTCs solutions.

3. Results
All samples attained high density values, higher than 99% of theoretical density (TD) except ZrB2 which was 95.8%. Theoretical density was calculated using the rule of mixtures. Table 2 shows SPS conditions and density values measured after sintering. Phase analysis by XRD confirmed all starting phases remained in UHTCs after SPS.
Fig. 1 shows secondary electron (SE) images of ground, polished and etched UHTCs surfaces after SPS. ZrB2 are the larger grey grains (2-6 𝛍 m), finer dark grains are SiC (1-2 𝛍 m) and light grey grains are La2O3 ( ∼ 1 𝛍 m) difficult to distinguish from ZrB2 grains, which are, respectively, labelled with 1, 2, and 3 in Fig. 1c as they were confirmed with EDS. Homogeneous dispersion of SiC in ZS20 and SiC/La2O3 in ZS20La is found in the ZrB2 matrix. SiC and La2O3 are usually located together forming small agglomerates as an interconnected network in the ZrB2 and HfB2 matrix. However, less than 5% of SiC and La2O3 particles were detected separately. On the other hand, SiC agglomerates as large as 20-30 𝛍 m were found in HfB2-based composites as seen in Fig. 1b indicated by an arrow.

3.3.2. ZS20La
ZS20La showed pure parabolic behavior at 1400 ◦ C ( n ∼ 2) and its oxidation kinetic exponent increases with temperature to
∼ 2.5 at 1500 ◦ C. In addition, values of n for mass gain and oxide scale thickness at 1600 ◦ C are different by almost four times, 2.1 for mass gain and 7.5 for oxide scale, which indicates that an oxygen adsorption mechanism may be active because large changes in mass gain lead to small changes in oxide scale. Oxide scale thickness of ZS20La is smaller than oxide scale thickness of ZS20 at 1400 ◦ C while the converse applies for 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C as seen in Fig. 2, presumably due to the higher volatilization of B2O3 present in the BS layer of ZS20 at these temperatures. Therefore, ZS20La is more oxidation resistant at 1400 ◦ C but not at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C. The main difference between the oxide layers of ZS20 and ZS20La is the mixing of the BS glassy melt with the ZrO2 scale which is more viscous in ZS20La and does not ascend directly to the top surface, producing a more homogeneous mixing between glassy and oxide phase as seen if Fig. 7 is compared to the ZS20 oxide layer in Fig. 5a or b. Homogeneous mixing of BS melt with ZrO2 could be detrimental for the system as diffusion through ZrO2 is much quicker than through a BS melt even if it has higher viscosity due to the presence of La2O3. La2O3 proximity to SiC promotes the inclusion of La2O3 into the BS phase, increasing viscosity and thermal stability as well as lowering the oxygen diffusion coefficient through the glassy phase. In fact, La2O3 is used as an additive to increase creep resistance of MoSi2, 45 which contains glassy SiO2 at grain boundaries and triple junctions. question: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited what high-temperature strength at 1800°C, and how did this compare to the undoped version?
answer: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited a high-temperature strength of 390 ± 21 MPa at 1800°C. This compared to the undoped version, which had a high-temperature strength of 34 ± 6 MPa at 1800°C [7].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.

3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
Fig. 7 shows the 1800 ◦ C-flexural strength versus sintering temperature of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics with and without C dopant, compared with several typical ZrB2  and HfB2-based UHTCs. In the previous studies, in order to obtain superior high-temperature properties, some refractory materials have been considered to be added into the matrix. The most widely  used  refractory  phases  were  ZrC  [15,40]  and  SiC  [13,38].  In addition, high-temperature resistant phases such as WC [41 -43], B4C [44],  Mo2C  [45]  and  SiB6 [46]  were  also  considered.  For  superior strength at ultra-high temperatures, it is generally not recommended to employ sintering additives with low melting point. Consequently, the sintering temperature of these reported ZrB2-based UHTCs with superior high-temperature strength typically exceeds 1800 ◦ C, resulting in significant energy expenditure. In contrast, introducing sintering additives with low melting point into ZrB2 ceramics can significantly reduce the densification  temperature,  however  the  residual  lower  melting  point phases would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs (e.g., the strength of ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2 at 1800 ◦ C is only 30 MPa). In the current work, active carbon was added into the ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic exhibited excellent flexural strength (390 MPa) at 1800 ◦ C, surpassing that of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC [15,40], ZrB2-30 vol% SiC [38], HfB2-20 vol% SiB6  [46], and HfB2-5 vol% SiC-5 vol% WC [41]. 1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics. 4. Conclusion
In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

In contrast, the ZS15 specimen yielded at only approximately 30 MPa, and then steadily deformed with a large strain at a low stress of 30 -34 MPa. The low yield strength was attributed to the presence of Zr -Si phases with a low-melting point. It has been reported that the low melting point phases would significantly reduce the  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs  [38,39].  After flexural test, the ZS15 specimen was obviously bent and not completely broken into two pieces, as displayed in Fig. 6(c). The high-temperature flexural strength of ZC1600-2h was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was an order of  magnitude higher than ZS15 (34 ± 6 MPa), as shown in Table 2. Compared  to  its  room-temperature  strength  of  494 ± 2  MPa,  the high-temperature strength retention ratio (the ratio of 1800 ◦ C-strength to RT-strength) of ZC1600-2h was as high as ~79%, which was much higher than that of ZS15 (~6%).

4.2.2. Mechanical behavior
The flexural strength of hot pressed ZrB2 tested between room temperature and 1600 ◦ C showed a value of 360 MPa at room temperature decreasing to a minimum of 170 MPa. The same ceramic when tested in Ar  between  1500 ◦ C  and  2300 ◦ C  raised  its  strength  to  300  MPa  at 2200 ◦ C, which then lowered down to 220 MPa at 2300 ◦ C [151]. A historic study by Rhodes [143] demonstrated the increase in bending strength  of  ZrB2  from  325  MPa  at  room  temperature  to  420  MPa  at 800 ◦ C. Further, increase in temperature reduced the strength to 145 MPa at 1400 ◦ C.  Bend strength again raised to 200 MPa at 1900 ◦ C followed by a drop at 2200 ◦ C to 50 MPa. The initial rise in bend strength was ascribed to the thermal residual stresses relieving at higher temperatures. The second rise in bend strength was attributed to the plastic flow from creep, again relieving the stresses [143]. ZrB2  ceramic composite (with 5 vol % WC + 3 vol % SiC) developed by Silvestroni et al. [152] retained a flexural strength of 836 MPa at 1800 ◦ C. The elevated temperature bend strength of some ZrB2  and HfB2  based ceramics is illustrated  in  Fig.  18.  Particulate  reinforced  di-borides  especially  SiC

4. Discussion
ceramics studied to date (217 MPa at 1800 ◦ C) for matrix grain size of 8 𝛍 m. However, plastic deformation occurred at 1800 ◦ C for matrix grain sizes lower than 5 𝛍 m. Elastic behaviour with relatively high strength was found with increasing ZrB2 grain size introducing a microstructural tailoring factor that controls the high temperature mechanical response. A systematic study of grain size effect and SiC content on high temperature strength of ZrB2 based ceramics is required to understand the mechanical responses and failure mechanisms of these systems. In addition, this is an effect that has been characterised for room temperature strength. The effect of sintering method on mechanical properties has been analysed. Pressureless sintered ZrB2-20 MoSi2 had higher strength at 1500 ◦ C than hot-pressed ZrB2-20 MoSi2 while the room temperature strengths remained similar. In addition, hot-pressed ZrB2-15 MoSi2 and SPSed ZrB2-15 MoSi2 have similar room and high temperature strength. This effect is explained in terms of the secondary phases formed during hot-pressing and SPS of these materials, such as SiO2 and ZrO2 which soften the grain boundaries at high temperatures, especially the residual silica pockets. The addition of carbon to the system avoided the residual silica pocket formation during hot-pressing increasing substantially high temperature strength of ZrB2-15 MoSi2. In the present study, the general trend is that HfB2-based UHTCs had higher flexural strength at high temperature than ZrB2-based UHTCs (Tables 6 and 7). The strength at 1400 ◦ C of ZS20 and ZS20La and HS20 and HS20La is approximately twice that of ZrB2 and HfB2, 37 respectively. The reduction of the strength at 1400 ◦ C in the La2O3-doped UHTCs could be attributed to La2O3 inducing grain boundary softening.

An investigation of results in other studies leads to the conclusion that although the grain size in ZrB2 ceramics plays an important role in the flexural strength, it is not of singular importance [31]. In summary, SPS processing of five different ZrB2 based compositions, with different amounts of SiC and ZrC added was performed at a relatively low temperature (1800 ◦ C) in a total time of 17 min. The results indicated that SiC and ZrC additions resulted in an enhancement in densification. A higher percentage of SiC results in grain growth inhibition, enhanced thermal conductivity, and increased Rockwell A hardness and strength. Further research is necessary to determine the effect of additional sintering parameters, such as heating rate, on the composites properties.

2.2. Hot-press sintering
The  ball-milled  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders  were  hot  pressed  at 1350 -1600 ◦ C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement,  load  and  temperature  were  recorded  using  TestMark (Fangrui  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.,  Changchun,  China)  to  analyze  the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate  of  10 ◦ C/min and then held for 30 min. When the temperature reached above 800 ◦ C, the uniaxial load began to be applied at a cross speed of 1 mm/min. After reaching 40 MPa, the uniaxial pressure was automatically maintained. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦ C, 1450 ◦ C, 1600 ◦ C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600,  respectively.  The  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics  sintered  at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350 -1600 ◦ C following the same sintering process mentioned above for comparison. The as-sintered ZrSi2 ceramics were labeled as Z1350, Z1450, Z1600.
