question: In Liu et al. (2023), how did the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolve from 1350°C to 1600°C during hot-press sintering, and what was the final state of the Zr-Si-C phases?
answer:
In Liu et al. (2023), the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolved as follows during hot-press sintering:

- At 1350°C, the polished surface of the ceramics was quite rough with a lot of pores, and the loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C was transformed into a porous Zr-Si-C cluster [1].
- When the sintering temperature increased to 1450°C, the pores became less, and the Zr-Si-C cluster precipitated spheres with a diameter of ~85 nm [1].
- After hot-sintering at 1600°C for 30 min, the Zr-Si-C cluster transformed into a core-shell phase, with the core being carbide enriched Zr and the shell being carbide enriched Si [1].
- After hot-sintering at 1600°C for 2 h, the Zr-Si-C phase was almost entirely transformed into SiC and ZrC [10].

The final state of the Zr-Si-C phases was that they were almost entirely transformed into SiC and ZrC [10].

chunks.text:
3.1. Phase composition and microstructure
Fig. 3 shows the typical morphologies on the polished surface of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at different temperatures. As can be seen in Fig. 3(a), the polished surface of ZC1350 was quite rough with a lot of pores. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450 ◦ C, the pores became less (Fig. 3(c)). In comparison, the polished surfaces of ZC1600 and ZC1600-2h were flat with almost no pores (Fig. 3(e, g)). Fig.  3(b,  d,  f,  h)  shows  the  high  magnification  images  to  reveal  the morphological  evolution.  After  hot-sintering  at  1350 ◦ C,  the  loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C was transformed into a porous Zr -Si -C cluster, as marked by the dotted ellipse in Fig. 3(b). Based on the reaction between the siliceous component and C (Reaction 4 -6), the Zr -Si -C cluster may contain ZrSi2,  ZrSi,  Si,  ZrC,  and  SiC.  It  is  difficult  to  distinguish  the various phases. With the sintering temperature increasing to 1450 ◦ C, the  Zr -Si -C cluster  precipitated  spheres  with  a  diameter  of  ~85  nm (Fig. 3(d)). After hot-sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 30 min, as shown in Fig. 3 (f), the Zr -Si -C cluster tfransformed into a core-shell phase (as marked by the dotted lines). Based on the micro-area analysis (inserted image in Fig. 3(f)), the core was carbide enriched Zr, and the shell was carbide enriched Si. The reactivity of Zr species with carbonaceous materials is higher than that of Si species, as can be confirmed in the erosion of carbon  fiber  by  molten  ZrSi2 [35,36],  tending  to  form  a  similar core-shell  structure  [37]. In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

2.2. Hot-press sintering
The  ball-milled  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders  were  hot  pressed  at 1350 -1600 ◦ C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement,  load  and  temperature  were  recorded  using  TestMark (Fangrui  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.,  Changchun,  China)  to  analyze  the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate  of  10 ◦ C/min and then held for 30 min. When the temperature reached above 800 ◦ C, the uniaxial load began to be applied at a cross speed of 1 mm/min. After reaching 40 MPa, the uniaxial pressure was automatically maintained. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦ C, 1450 ◦ C, 1600 ◦ C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600,  respectively.  The  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics  sintered  at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350 -1600 ◦ C following the same sintering process mentioned above for comparison. The as-sintered ZrSi2 ceramics were labeled as Z1350, Z1450, Z1600.

1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.

Reaction Path and Synthesis Mechanism
An initial series of sintering experiments examined the reactive path and synthesis mechanism of the Zr-B 4 C-Si system without using SHS reactions to obtain densified products. The processing parameters and final phase compositions corresponding to chemical reaction (10) after a series of pressureless heat treatments were shown in Table 1. Stacked XRD patterns of samples prepared under the different heat treatment conditions were presented in Figure 3. In sample 1, apart from Zr, B 4 C and Si in raw powders, X-ray diffraction also identified cubic ZrC 1-x and ZrO 2 phases. The ZrO 2 phase was attributed to the oxidation of Zr powder in air and ZrO 2 milling balls. The presence of ZrC 1-x indicated that the reaction between Zr and B 4 C commenced at 800 °C and ZrC 1-x formed preferentially, which was inconsistent with the results of thermodynamic calculations (Figure 2). The formation of ZrC as shown above was mainly due to non-stoichiometric ZrC 1-x , although the Gibbs energy of ZrB 2 was lower than that of ZrC. There was no apparent difference between the XRD patterns of samples 1 and 2. However, it was found that the 1 h hold at 800 °C delayed the hot explosion temperature from 980 to 1050 °C under a 10 °C/min heating rate. This phenomenon demonstrated that many more atoms diffused during the temperature hold, especially between Zr/C and Zr/B.

A B S T R A C T
As shown in [13 -15], adding graphite inclusions is one of the most effective ways to enhance toughness and thermal shock resistance of brittle materials. One of the ways to introduce such inclusions can be the reactive hot pressing of ZrC, B4C, and Si precursors [16], which is based on the reaction between the metal carbide and B4C [17], with the subsequent interaction of a part of the released carbon with silicon. While the approach is being successfully applied to the TiB2-SiC-C system [18], it turned out not to be as efficient for the HfB2-SiC-C ceramic composite. As is shown in [19], the reactive hot pressing of HfC, B4C, and Si precursors  resulted  in  macroscopic  stratification  of  the  samples.  The applicability of the reactive sintering approach for the ZrB2-SiC-C system has not yet been reported. Another possible way to obtain SiC in the final microstructure is by introducing silicon atoms into ZrC and B4C precursors through ZrSi2  [20,21]. An essential difference between the two ways for reaction sintering procedure is that the melting point of silicon  (1414 º C)  is  lower  than  the  temperature  of  ZrB2 and  SiC

3.1.1. ZrC-B4C-Si (R1) series
As was shown in our previous work [16], the densification of the ZrC-B4C-Si  powder  mixture  during  hot  pressing  (Fig.  6a)  proceeds through three main stages. The first stage, providing about 30 % of the overall  sample  shrinkage, occurs  during  the sample  heating between 1000 ◦ C and 1300 ◦ C, alongside the precursor phase content decreasing by about 50 % and reaction (1) products (ZrB2 and SiC) appearing. This stage was explained by the reaction-induced powder mobility, arising from the disappearance of the initial phase grains (B4C, ZrC, and Si) alongside  the  formation  of  new  ones  (ZrB2,  SiC,  and  graphite).  The shrinkage stops at approximately 1300 ◦ C, and the sample height remains  constant  up  to  1550 ◦ C.  During  this  second  stage,  called the plateau ,  reaction  (1) is  completed  by  about  90 %,  meaning  that  the precursor phase content is now 10 % as compared to the beginning. As we can see from Fig. 6, presenting the material structure before the start of the first stage (Fig. 6b) and at the end of the second stage (Fig. 6c), the reaction (1) leads to a significant structure refinement with most of the grains after the second stage significantly less than 0.5 µ m. It should be noted that stratification was not observed either after the first or the second stage. The third stage began at 1600 ◦ C, with shrinkage occurring even faster than in the first stage, and continued until 1850 ◦ C. In [16], we suggested that the rapid consolidation at this stage originated from new phase plastic deformation and was facilitated by the rapid growth of nanostructured nuclei, the presence of molten silicon remnants, and the completion  of  reaction  (1).  This  is  the  stage  when  the  stratification occurs.

With the temperature increasing higher than 1410 ◦ C, the solid Si transited into Si-rich liquid phases, which were extruded from the Zr -Si -C clusters into the small residual pores among ZrB2 particles under applied pressure (Fig. 5 (c)), contributing to the second rapid densification stage of 1420 -1600 ◦ C (Fig. 5(f)). Due to the extrusion of the Si-rich liquid phase from the Zr -Si -C cluster, a core-shell phase with a Zr-rich interior core and a Si-rich exterior shell was formed, as shown in Figs. 5(d) and Fig. 3 (f). Then, the large core-shell phase gradually split into small ZrC/SiC particles(Fig. 5(e)). After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h, the Zr -Si -C phase was almost entirely transformed into SiC and ZrC. Correspondingly, the densification was further enhanced with the relative density of ZC16002h of 100%. In short, in this way, the sintering additive of ZrSi2 not only promoted  the  densification  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs,  but  also  was  converted into high-temperature resistant phases by C dopant, which would be benefit to lower sintering temperature and improve high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs.

Microstructural investigation of the bulk ceramic specimens (Fig. 10) demonstrated  that  the  increase  in  relative  density  of  ceramics  was attained without any notable grain growth. The ceramics consisted of the boride matrix and TaSi2 inclusions. In all investigated specimens, the peculiar  microgradient  structure  was  present,  corroborating  earlier findings [30]. The microgradient structure could be formed during the hot pressing of combustion products via the following possible mechanism. The ZrB2 layer was formed on the surface of (Zr,Ta)B2 grains as a result of grain boundary diffusion. Mutual diffusion between (Zr,Ta)B2 and the ZrB2  interlayer took place inside the diboride grains simultaneously with the growth of the grains. However, the activation energy of diffusion in the crystal lattice of ZrB2 is ~700 kJ/mol, while that of grain boundary diffusion is much lower (~ 240 kJ/mol) [30,81,82].

5. Conclusions
ZrB2-SiC based ultra-high temperature ceramics with addition of ZrO2 were prepared by hot-pressing. By means of the water-quenching, thermal shock behavior of the ceramics was investigated with temperature difference increased from 200 to 800 ◦ C. Results of residual strength and critical temperature difference revealed that ZS10Z exhibited the optimal thermal shock resistance. Simulated analysis with FEM found that ZS10Z provided lowest thermal stress and stress intensity factors during thermal shock, which was in a good agreement with the experimental data. Improved toughness was beneficial for the thermal shock resistance. In addition, phase transformation from t-ZrO2 to m-ZrO2 during thermal shock led to the tougher surface, which was also favorable for the thermal shock resistance. With further addition of ZrO2 to ZrB2-SiC, phase transformation during thermal shock will crack the surface. In conclusion, ZrB2 -10 vol%SiC-10 vol%ZrO2 wasdiscovered to provide the best thermal shock resistance, which should be instructive for the design of ZrB2 -based ultra-high temperature ceramics.

4. Conclusions
All the UHTCMCs successfully passed the base qualification and cyclic  exposure for three times, achieving temperatures in the order of 2000 -2500 K depending on the material and corresponding emissivity. Larger amounts of SiC were conducive in maintaining the Near InfraRed (NIR) spectral emissivity around 0.6 -0.8, owing to continuous recall of silica-glass to the surface, which all contributed not to exceed 2050 K during the qualification test. On the other hand, when only 10 vol% SiC was  present  in  the  matrix,  the  NIR  emissivity  waved  in  the  0.8 -0.4 range, owing to a prevalence of zirconium oxide on the surface. For these set of composites, regardless the presence of short or long fibre, the temperature  approached  2500 K.  The  reference  Cf/SiC  loaded  with 10 vol%  ZrB2 composite,  featured  by  mostly  silica,  had  a  spectral emissivity as high as 0.8 -0.9 with stable peak temperature of 2100 K. Although such discrepancies in the experienced thermal histories, the new  UHTCMCs  outperformed  the  reference  composite  by  enhanced ablation resistance.
Reusability tests, repeated three times, were first very reproducible assessing the reliability of the method, and then confirmed the trend of the baseline qualification outcomes, verifying the capability of the novel UHTCMCs to maintain their functionality and structural integrity after repeated exposure, making them appealing for future reusable TPS.
Finally, an extremely demanding test condition was selected in order to assess the materials performance under the peak pressure conditions of  an  abort  re-entry  scenario.  This  was  achieved  by  substantially increasing  the  heat  flux  delivered  to  the  surface,  up  to  8.5 MW/m 2 . Despite the considerable erosion and the very high temperature reached (over 3000 K for all UHTCMC compositions), which even led to material melting on the exposed surface, all the UHTCMCs were able to preserve a good  structural  integrity,  without  any  catastrophic  failures,  demonstrating their ability to properly protect the spacecraft even in the most challenging re-entry environment.

Fabrication, Microstructure, and Properties of Zirconium Diboride Matrix Ceramic
The isothermal oxidation of the ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic was performed in static air at constant temperatures of 1000±15 ºC, 1200±15 ºC and 1400±15 ºC for 30 min. Compared with the original strength of 580 MPa, the strengths for the specimen after oxidation for 30 min at 1000 ºC, 1200 ºC and 1400 ºC were 609 MPa, 656 MPa and 660 MPa, respectively, due to the sealing of the flaws on the surface of the specimen by the oxide layer. The thermal shock resistances of the specimens before and after oxidation were measured by water quenching. The measured Δ T crit values for the specimen after oxidization at 1000 ºC, 1200 ºC and 1400 ºC were 352 °C, 453 ºC and 623 ºC, respectively, which were distinctly higher than the temperature of 270 °C for the unoxidized specimen. The improvement in thermal shock resistance was attributed to the formation of the oxide layers. The formation of oxide layers resulted in the following behaviors: (I) the healing of surface flaws, (II) an increase in flexural strength, (III) the formation of a compressive stress zone beneath the surface oxide layers, (IV) a decrease in thermal stress and (V) the consumption of thermal stress. These five aspects improved the thermal shock resistance of ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic. The results indicate the potential of this method for improving the strength and thermal shock resistance of ZrB 2 -based ceramics.

4. Results
The numerical simulations were carried out in the range of altitudes between 80 and 20 km of the reentry trajectory.
The vehicle structure is assumed to be initially at a temperature of 300 K. The aerodynamic and the structure thermal analysis are performed using the 'coupled' and the 'decoupled' methods described in Section 3.
Figs. 7(a), (b) show the fluid and solid temperature distributions obtained with the fully coupled approach, after 30 s and 60 s, respectively. The corresponding altitudes along the reentry trajectory are 45 and 20 km. The temperature inside the UHTC structure gradually increases with time, achieving the maximum values in the nose region, where convective heat fluxes are larger. After 60 s, the maximum temperature in the solid is about 1600 K.
Figs. 8(a), (b) show the temperature distribution in the solid structure, at the same conditions as in Figs. 7, obtained using the decoupled approach and a time interval /Delta1t = 1 s. The temperature contours shown in Fig. 8 do not exhibit appreciable differences with respect to the fully coupled computations, even if the required computational time is lower.
The stagnation point convective heat fluxes and temperature profiles along the trajectory are shown in Figs. 9(a), (b). The maximum stagnation point heat flux, attained at t = 55 s, is about 3.5 MW m -2 , corresponding to an altitude of about 22 km, while maximum temperature is reached after about 60 s ( z = 18 km). The peak temperature, corresponding to the peak heat flux, is about 1600 K; this value is about 800 K lower than the local radiative equilibrium temperature (i.e. the temperature corresponding to zero conduction in the solid) and also about 300 K lower than the overall radiative equilibrium taking into account the conductive flux across the solid (see Fig. 9). 2.2. Atmospheric Re-entry
Starting at a 120 km altitude and an initial ~ Mach 25 velocity, NASA space shuttles were required to end missions by landing on a runway 91 m wide [33], - a challenge for blunt-shaped bodies. The accuracy of a body during re-entry is related to leading edge geometry. Sharp geometries have higher ballistic coefficients (BC) and lower drag forces/coefficients than blunt geometries. However, as BC increases, so too do maximum heating rates. If a material can withstand ultra-high temperatures, the BC of the vehicle can be increased via sharper, more streamlined geometries that allow several advantages, like significantly altering the altitude of maximum deceleration. The lower the altitude of deceleration, the shorter the amount of time spent in the atmosphere, or the more rapidly the vehicle arrives at the surface of the Earth. The benefits of a high BC compared to a low BC are listed in Table 2.
Opeka et al. indicated the 500 ◦ -2000 ◦ C range as being the necessary materials window for hypersonics [34]. Technically, hypersonic velocity is defined as any speed exceeding the speed of sound, but is typically more  specifically  defined  as  beginning  at  Mach  5,  or  in  the  range 1702-3403 m/s. Hypersonic velocities are less demanding the further from the Earth ' s atmosphere due to decreasing air density. The Space Shuttle re-entering the Earth ' s  atmosphere at nearly Mach 25 in low earth  orbit  is  a  substantially  different  thermal,  aerodynamic,  and oxidation scenario than Mach 25 a few kilometers above the surface.
As discussed earlier, the maximum usage temperature for any SiC based thermal protection system (TPS) is ~1727 ◦ C and for comparative purposes, SiC is viewed as protective up to1727 ◦ C. 1. Introduction
After  a  decade  of  research  on  ultra  high  temperature  ceramics (UHTCs), ZrB2 based materials have emerged as the potential candidate for  extreme  environments  associated  with  the  hypersonic  futuristic technology [1 -3]. ZrB2 based materials have high melting point ( > 3000 0 C [4]), high thermal conductivity (85 -90 W/m/K at room temperature [5]) and strength retention at high temperature [6,7]. These properties of  ZrB2 based  materials  conform  to  the  properties  of  the  materials required for the intended applications [1]. SiC has been often used with ZrB2 to make ZrB2 -SiC composites [4 -8]. However, the monolithic ZrB2 and ZrB2 -SiC composites have some limitations for practical application due to low fracture toughness and poor thermal shock resistance. The fracture toughness of the monolithic ZrB2 values ranges from 2 to 4 MPa m 1/2 , and increases marginally to 3.5 -5.5 MPa m 1/2 with SiC addition, i. e. for the ZrB2 -SiC composites [8]. Thermal shock severity ( Δ Tc) was reported to be 275 and 400 ◦ C for the monolithic ZrB2  and ZrB2 - 20% SiC composite, respectively [9]. This restricts the applicability of ZrB2 based  materials  to  large  structures.  Various  test  articles  of  ZrB2 -SiC composites after flight and arc jet tests were found damaged severely [10].  To  overcome  these  limitations,  UHTCs  require  conversion  to  a more damage tolerant material. One way to produce damage tolerant

1. Introduction
The design of hypersonic vehicles needs leading edge components with sharp profile to improve flight performances like drag reduction, easier manoeuvrability, or larger cross-range options during atmospheric exit and re-entry [1]. At some stage of an hypersonic (re-entry) flight, the aforementioned sharp components are subject of very stressful heat fluxes in corrosive plasmas from atmosphere, and temperatures exceeding 2300 K for several minutes may become for these components a projected service condition [2]. Such extreme conditions limit the spectrum of possible candidates that nowadays are intensively searched within the class of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC). UHTC typically include non-oxides with melting/decomposition temperatures in excess of 3300 K. Diborides of the group IV transition metals like ZrB2 and HfB2 (3518 K and 3653 K of melting point, respectively) are currently the base systems taken in greater consideration to answer this technological challenge. Besides melting points greater than 3500 K [3], transition metal diborides have also thermal conductivity above 50 W/(m K) up to 1500 K [4], giving them a great potential advantage over more traditional materials to operate in extreme environments such as those experienced

Thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-10vol%SiC ultra-high temperature ceramics with addition of ZrO2 was investigated by means of water-quenching as well as the finite element analysis. Optimal residual strength and critical temperature difference were obtained from ZrB2-10 vol%SiC ceramic added by 10vol%ZrO2. Simulated results revealed that ZrB2-10 vol%SiC-10 vol%ZrO2 provided the lowest thermal stress and stress intensity factor during thermal shock, which confirmed the experimental data. Such improvement in thermal shock resistance compared with ZrB2-10vol%SiC was primarily ascribed to the improved toughness by the addition of ZrO2. Besides, appropriate phase transformation of ZrO2 during quenching was further beneficial to resist the thermal shock.

In the case of coatings and graded materials, theoretical and simulated progress on the thermal shock, especially the thermal stress intensity factors (SIFs), was also carried out [14]. With regard to UHTCs, F. Monteverde et al. developed thermal shock of ZrB2-15vol%SiC ceramic, as well as ZrB2-15vol%SiC doped with 10vol%HfB2 [15]. And the critical temperature difference of both ceramics was further analyzed according to the residual strength after thermal shock. In addition, only a few studies on the thermal shock of UHTCs can be found [16-18].
The present paper focused on the thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-SiC based UHTCs with addition of zirconia. By means of traditional water-quenching as well as the finite element method (FEM), influence of ZrO2 on the thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-SiC ceramics was analyzed.

This requirement has led the scienti /uniFB01 c community to develop high strength UHTCs components, as high as 1 GPa at room temperature (RT) [18,19]. Moreover, SiC reinforced MeB2 have shown the highest RT strength values [20]. However, UHTCs must withstand extreme conditions of temperature and stress under harsh environments and the available data about high temperature mechanical properties, i.e. strength and creep resistance, is scarce. Concerning HT strength measurements, there have been few reports of the strength of UHTCs at the expected operating temperatures. ZrB2 shows a strength value of ∼ 220 MPa in the temperature range from 1600 °C to 2200 °C [21]. Hu and Wang reported a strength of ∼ 220 MPa for ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC at 1800 °C [22] while Neuman et al. found a strength of 540 MPa at 1800 °C and 260 MPa at 2200 °C in ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% B4C illustrating the impact on strength of a small amount of B4C [23]. Highest strength at high temperature till date, 290 MPa at 2200 °C and 2300 °C, was reported by Neuman et al. in ZrB2-10 vol.% ZrC [24]. However, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol.% ZrC at 1800 °C was 345 MPa which is lower than the 540 MPa of ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% B4C at 1800 °C. All the strengths mentioned above were measured in inert atmosphere. However, measured strengths in air for ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% B4C were ∼ 360 MPa at 1500 and 1600 °C [25], much lower than the 540 MPa shown at 1800 °C in Ar [23].

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Not only the mechanical properties and oxidation resistance, but responses of materials to the conditions of high enthalpy plasma flows that are typically encountered during atmospheric re-entry need to be evaluated. One of the possible ways to investigate such effects, in terms of materials' response to large heat fluxes as well as oxidation resistance under extreme/complex conditions, is Arc-jet testing. A schematic representation of a typical arc-jet testing set-up is presented in Figure 14a. In a recent work, Savino et al. (Savino, 2008) had performed arc-jet testing using a 80 kW plasma torch, operating under in inert gas conditions at mass flow rates up to 5 g/s, on hemispheric shaped HfB 2 - 5 vol.% MoSi 2 and HfC - 5 vol.% MoSi 2 specimens placed at a distance of 6 cm form the exit torch (see Figure 14bd).  The  temperatures of investigation were between 1950 o C and 2400 o C, which revealed that both the  materials  could  be  stable  up  to  temperatures  exceeding  2000 o C. Prior to this work, arc-jet testing were done only on ZrB 2 -  SiC and HfB 2 -  SiC composites (Gasch, 2004). Due to the issues associated with experimental determination of such high temperature properties, a lot of work has focused on theoretical simulation of the behavior via computational fluid dynamics (Borrelli, 2010), FEM based analysis (Borrelli, 2009; Stine, 1968) or various other computational tools. Such models need to simulate the demands for the application under consideration. However, despite the progress with experimental and computational facilities, still lacking  is  a  comprehensive  thermostructural  database  for  the  various  potential UHTCs.

1. Introduction
ZrB2-based composites have an extremely high melting point (>3000 /C176 C), high thermal and electrical conductivities, low density, and excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [1-4]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramic composites have been considered as potential candidates for application in critical thermal protection systems and other components of future hypersonic aircraft or re-entry vehicles [1-7]. Silicon carbide (SiC) is commonly used as an additive to improve the oxidation resistance and mechanical properties of ZrB2. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC particulate composites (ZS) are regarded as the baseline material in this research. However, unsatisfactory thermal shock resistance remains the obstacle to the development of ZrB2-SiC ceramic on an industrial scale. To avoid or mitigate catastrophic failure of ZrB2SiC ceramics during thermal shock, most current researches focus on composition design to improve the performance by selecting different additives [8-12]. Carbon is also sometimes used as an additive to enhance thermal shock resistance [12-14]. Recently, Levine et al. [13] studied the mechanical properties of ZrB214 vol% SiC-30 vol% C. They concluded that the addition of carbon could reduce strength but increases fracture toughness. Hong et al. [14] fabricated ZrB2-SiC-graphite composite, which exhibits increased thermal shock resistance compared with monolithic ZrB2-SiC. The toughness and hence the reliability and thermal

THERMAL SHOCK RESISTANCE
After the recent tragic explosion of Space Shuttle Columbia, the findings of the Columbia Accident Investigation Board and the recent Space Exploration Initiative suggest that safety be made the top priority in the design of new reentry vehicles (Wang et al., 2007). During reentry from Low Earth Orbit (LEO), such as from the Space Station or parking orbits, relatively high speeds (on the order of 8 km/s) are reached (Monteverde et al., 2007). External thermal protection systems of reusable space vehicles, in the form of rigid surfaces in areas of ultra-high working temperatures (beyond 1800 °C), must be able to withstand high temperatures, high heat flux and mechanical stresses associated with vibrations at launch and re-entry in Earth's atmosphere (Tang et al., 2007). Most ceramic matrix composites, including ZrB 2 matrix ceramics, have inherently low fracture toughness and are thus susceptible to catastrophic fractures caused by thermal stresses under the above-mentioned conditions. Consequently, the thermal shock resistance of the ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic must be estimated.
In  the  present  work,  the  thermal  shock  resistance  of  the  ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic was investigated by the water quenching method, and the microstructure of the specimen before and after water quenching was observed. Furthermore, the temperature and thermal stress distributions in the specimen during instantaneous water quenching were simulated and analyzed in detail by finite element analysis.

Fig. 3b shows surface crack of ZS after thermal shock at w 500 /C14 C/s. As can be seen, the crack is induced at the center of the specimens, and the cracks is along x (marked by red arrow), which can be attributed to the temperature gradient along x direction (tension thermal stress along y ) at macro-level. Micro-level thermal stress, caused by thermal expansion difference of different phases, is tensional stress at interface between SiC and ZrB2 matrix when temperature > 1950 /C14 C (fabrication temperature). There is assumed no thermal stress at the interface between SiC and ZrB2 matrix at 1950 /C14 C. The interface is assumed the first failure place, as thermal stress caused by different thermal expansion of SiC and ZrB2 exists.

question: Comparing porous YB2C2 ceramics at about 50% porosity fabricated by Yang et al. 2025 with ZrB2-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites after 200 thermal shock cycles fabricated by Huang et al. 2016: which material system reports the higher room-temperature strength value?
answer: The room-temperature strength of porous YB2C2 ceramics at about 50% porosity fabricated by Yang et al. 2025 is reported to be 76.7 MPa [8]. However, the room-temperature strength of ZrB2-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites after 200 thermal shock cycles fabricated by Huang et al. 2016 is not provided in the sources. Therefore, the comparison cannot be made based on the available information.

3.3. Compressive behavior of porous YB2C2  samples
Finally,  benchmarking  against  other  porous  UHTCs,  as  shown  in Fig. 15 and Table 5, highlights the competitiveness of porous YB2C2. The expected decline of compressive strength with porosity is observed, yet our  PEP  plus in-situ boro/carbothermal reduction sintering  route  delivers  substantially  higher  strengths  at  comparable  porosities.  For example, at 57 -75 % porosity, the strengths reported by Chen et al. for partially  sintered  porous  YB2C2 fall  in  the  34.78 -9.32 MPa  range, whereas the present samples exceed these values over the same porosity window [38]. These results underscore the efficacy of the single-material, single-step PEP strategy in building continuous, long-range lamellae that elevate the compressive performance of porous ceramics.

1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics. In recent years, slurry injection technology has demonstrated potential for shortening process cycles in SI by directly introducing high-solid-content ceramic slurries into fiber preforms. 15-18 According to the study by Liu et al., 19 the process was optimized through the injection of ZrB2 -phenolic resin slurry, achieving a density of 2.10 ± 0.05 g/cm 3 and porosity of approximately 40%. However, subsequent densification processes are required after slurry injection into preforms. Venkatachalam et al. 20 fabricated C f -ZrB 2 -Cm composites using slurry injection combined with radio-frequency-assisted CVI, achieving 90% relative density for C f -ZrB 2 within 24 h. Under oxyacetylene ablation, the composites exhibited mass ablation rate of 1.7 mg/s and linear ablation rate of 1.6 µ m/safter 60 s (peak temperature ∼ 2600 K). However, residual 10% porosity in the 2.5D carbon fiber preform, caused by complex pore structures and CVI process limitations, significantly reduced ablation performance. Yang et al. 21 obtained C/ZrB2-SiC with 86% relative density and 215 MPa flexural strength using 30 vol.% ZrB 2 slurry injection combined with 12 PIP cycles. Hu et al. 22 achieved a 286 MPa flexural strength for Cf /ZrC-SiC through eight PIP cycles. Although PIP enables gradient distribution control of matrix phases, its requirement for multiple cycles (typically > 10) extends fabrication time to hundreds of hours. 23 RMI rapidly produces high-density ceramic matrix composites through molten metal reactions with preforms. It is particularly suitable for complex-shaped components, having demonstrated efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and high application potential. 24,25

Porous ceramics with engineered anisotropy have attracted significant  attention  for  their  unique  combination  of  low  density,  tailored permeability, and direction-dependent mechanical and thermal properties, which are critical for advanced aerospace, energy, and environmental  applications  [1 -3]. For  instance,  YB2C2 retains  more  than  70 %  of  its room-temperature bending strength at 1300 ◦ C, and has demonstrated excellent chemical compatibility and enhanced mechanical performance

2.2. Characterization
Thermo  gravimetric  and  differential  scanning  calorimetry  experiments were carried out on a thermal analyzer (TG-DSC, STA 449F5, Netzsch, Germany) to determine the thermal debinding temperature of the polymer binders according to the weight loss. The solvent debinding green bodies approximately 2 mg were heated from room temperature to 800 ◦ C at a rate of 10 ◦ C/min under a flowing air atmosphere. The chemical and structural compositions of green bodies and brown bodies were determined using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker, Germany). The phase composition of porous YB2C2  ceramics was analyzed using an X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 advanced, Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) with Cu K α radiation. A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Merlin Compact, Zeiss, Germany) equipped with an energy dispersive  spectroscopy  (EDS)  detector  was  used  to  observe  the morphology of the samples. Compression tests  were performed  on  a universal  testing  machine  (UTM-50kN,  Zhejiang  Dana  Automation Technology Co. LTD., China). The average maximum load at failure of five  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  with  the  size  of  10 × 10 × 10 mm 3 was recorded as the compressive strength under a uniaxial compressive load at a crosshead speed of 0.5 mm/min. The grain structure and crystallographic  texture  orientation  maps  of  the  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  in different directions (the X-Y surface and the X-Z surface) were determined using Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD, Oxford Symmetry 22, Voltage 20 kV, diaphragm 120 μ m). Samples for EBSD observation were subjected to standard metallographic procedures including cutting,  grinding  and  polishing.  Laser  confocal  microscopy  (VK-1000, Japan) was used to test the roughness of the samples, and DIC was used to test changes in the mechanical behavior of the samples.

UHTCs based coatings (ZrB2 e SiC coating and ZrC e SiC coating) are prepared on the surface of PIP-C/SiC composites by slurry and CVD method, and the thermal stability is one of the key issues for C/SiC composites. This study aims to study thermal shock behavior of the coated composites. The results show the degradation of /uniFB02 exural strength without obvious variation, and the fracture mode and pull-out mechanism are also changed. The composition of coatings is characterized by XRD, SEM and XPS. The decrease of /uniFB02 exural strength during the tests is due to the debonding of the coating-matrix interfaces. Compared to uncoated C/SiC composites, the UHTCs based coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.

4. Conclusions
UHTCs based coatings (ZrB2 e SiC coating and ZrC e SiC coating) for PIP-C/SiC composites are deposited by slurry and CVD method, then the thermal shock behavior of the coated samples is studied, and the effect of thermal cycling on their /uniFB02 exural property and microstructures is discussed. The following conclusions have been reached:
- (1) ZrB2 e SiC (ZrC e SiC) coating is fabricated by slurry and CVD method with a thickness of 50 m m.
- (2) The coated samples showed the degradation of the /uniFB02 exural strength properties and no mass variation. Compared with the as-received C/SiC composites, the mode of the fracture behavior of coated C/SiC composites was changed, the samples exhibited an obvious brittle fracture behavior, and the coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.
- (3) ZrB2 e SiC coated C/SiC composites are no longer a singlephase of ZrB2, after thermal cycling tests, SiC and C are detected. The surface of ZrC e SiC coated C/SiC composites is no longer a single-phase of ZrC, SiC and C are also detected either.
- (4) The damage of the UHTCs based coatings caused by tensile stress could in /uniFB02 uence the mechanical properties of PIP-C/SiC composites under thermal cycling tests, and the /uniFB02 exural property of C/SiC composites is reduced.

Fabrication, Microstructure, and Properties of Zirconium Diboride Matrix Ceramic
The isothermal oxidation of the ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic was performed in static air at constant temperatures of 1000±15 ºC, 1200±15 ºC and 1400±15 ºC for 30 min. Compared with the original strength of 580 MPa, the strengths for the specimen after oxidation for 30 min at 1000 ºC, 1200 ºC and 1400 ºC were 609 MPa, 656 MPa and 660 MPa, respectively, due to the sealing of the flaws on the surface of the specimen by the oxide layer. The thermal shock resistances of the specimens before and after oxidation were measured by water quenching. The measured Δ T crit values for the specimen after oxidization at 1000 ºC, 1200 ºC and 1400 ºC were 352 °C, 453 ºC and 623 ºC, respectively, which were distinctly higher than the temperature of 270 °C for the unoxidized specimen. The improvement in thermal shock resistance was attributed to the formation of the oxide layers. The formation of oxide layers resulted in the following behaviors: (I) the healing of surface flaws, (II) an increase in flexural strength, (III) the formation of a compressive stress zone beneath the surface oxide layers, (IV) a decrease in thermal stress and (V) the consumption of thermal stress. These five aspects improved the thermal shock resistance of ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic. The results indicate the potential of this method for improving the strength and thermal shock resistance of ZrB 2 -based ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
Controlling crystallographic texture and pore architecture in porous ultra-high temperature ceramics is critical for tailoring their performance in extreme environments, yet achieving this dual control remains a formidable challenge.  Here,  we  demonstrate  a  novel  strategy  to  fabricate  architected  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  exhibiting strong crystallographic and mechanical property anisotropy by synergizing powder extrusion printing with in-situ reaction  synthesis.  The  core  of  our  approach  is  a  templated-growth  mechanism,  where  shear  forces  during extrusion  align  sacrificial  graphite  flakes  within  the  green  body.  These  aligned  flakes  subsequently  serve  as heterogeneous  nucleation  and  growth  sites  for  plate-like  YB2C2  grains  during  boro/carbothermal  reduction, effectively  translating  the  macro-scale  printed  path  into  a  micro-scale  crystallographic  texture.  This  process yields a remarkable hierarchical structure, featuring unidirectionally aligned pores and a strong [001] crystallographic texture parallel to the printing direction. The resulting ceramics exhibit a highly tunable porosity (50 -75 vol%) and exceptional compressive strength, reaching 76.7 ± 14.7 MPa at ~50 % porosity. Crucially, this microstructural control imparts a pronounced mechanical anisotropy, a key attribute for designing components with directionally dependent loads. This work establishes a new paradigm for manufacturing ultra-high temperature ceramics, enabling the concurrent design of geometry and crystallographic texture to achieve tailored thermomechanical properties.
when used as a reinforcement phase in Cu-based composites [5,6]. These properties position YB2C2 as an ideal material for structural components in harsh environments such as thermal protection systems, nuclear reactors, and hypersonic vehicle engines [7 -9]. However, realizing the full potential of porous YB2C2  ceramics hinges on the ability to precisely control  their  porosity,  crystallographic  texture,  and  macroscopic  geometry -a challenge that has yet to be fully addressed by conventional fabrication techniques.

3.3. Cycle dependence of thermal shock resistance
Fig. 7 shows the /uniFB02 exural strength of the C/C-ZrC composites experiencing di /uniFB00 erent number of thermal shock cycles from 1700 °C to the room temperature. The /uniFB02 exural strength decreases gradually with the increasing number of thermal shock cycles. The residual strength of the composite thermally shocked for 20 cycles is about 130 MPa, 70.3% of the as-produced C/C-ZrC composite. Mass change of the C/C-ZrC composite with di /uniFB00 erent number of thermal shock cycles was also measured and listed in Fig. 7. The mass loss keeps constant without obvious di /uniFB00 erence, which is the same as the composite thermally shocked at di /uniFB00 erent temperatures (< 1900 °C).
Fig. 7. Flexural strength and mass loss of the C/C-ZrC composites subjected to di /uniFB00 erent thermal shock cycles.
Fig. 8 shows the cross section microstructure of the C/C-ZrC composite thermally shocked from 1700 °C to the room temperature for di /uniFB00 erent thermal cycles. The formation of matrix cracks and interfacial debonding can be clearly observed as that shown in Fig. 5. With increasing numbers of thermal cycles, the interfacial debonding becomes more severe. The cross section microstructure of the composite thermally shocked for di /uniFB00 erent numbers is dense without formation of pores, which is consistent with the mass constant listed in Fig. 7. Here, the shocked temperature is merely 1700 °C, which is not high enough to enable the evaporation of the Zr2Si phase. Thus, no obvious weight change was measured.

4. Conclusion
Thermal shock resistance of the Zr-Si alloyed melt in /uniFB01 ltrated C/CZrC composite was tested using an induction heating equipment from ultra high temperatures (particularly > 1500 °C) to the room temperature. Both strength degradation and microstructure evolution were characterized in order to reveal the thermal shock induced damage to the composite. The following conclusions are obtained:
- (1) Flexural strength of the post thermal shocked C/C-ZrC composite slightly decreases initially without weight loss below 1300 °C, while it decreases with relatively high values and then sharply decreases over 1900 °C with obvious increase of weight loss.
- (2) Flexural strength of the post thermal shocked composites is dependence of thermal cycles. Flexural strength decreases gradually with the increasing number of thermal shocked cycles without obvious weight loss from 1700 °C to the room temperature.
- (3) Matrix cracking, interfacial debonding and matrix pores are the main reasons for the strength degradation of the composite thermally shocked for di /uniFB00 erent cycles and temperatures.

journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jeurceramsoc development of UHTCs able to withstand temperatures in excess of 2000 °C under the same stresses will be required. This requirement has led the scienti /uniFB01 c community to develop high strength UHTCs components, as high as 1 GPa at room temperature (RT) [18,19]. Moreover, SiC reinforced MeB2 have shown the highest RT strength values [20]. However, UHTCs must withstand extreme conditions of temperature and stress under harsh environments and the available data about high temperature mechanical properties, i.e. strength and creep resistance, is scarce. Concerning HT strength measurements, there have been few reports of the strength of UHTCs at the expected operating temperatures. ZrB2 shows a strength value of ∼ 220 MPa in the temperature range from 1600 °C to 2200 °C [21]. Hu and Wang reported a strength of ∼ 220 MPa for ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC at 1800 °C [22] while Neuman et al. found a strength of 540 MPa at 1800 °C and 260 MPa at 2200 °C in ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% B4C illustrating the impact on strength of a small amount of B4C [23]. Highest strength at high temperature till date, 290 MPa at 2200 °C and 2300 °C, was reported by Neuman et al. in ZrB2-10 vol.% ZrC [24]. However, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol.% ZrC at 1800 °C was 345 MPa which is lower than the 540 MPa of ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% B4C at 1800 °C. All the strengths mentioned above were measured in inert atmosphere. However, measured strengths in air for ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% B4C were ∼ 360 MPa at 1500 and 1600 °C [25], much lower than the 540 MPa shown at 1800 °C in Ar [23].

3.1. SiC whisker reinforcement
Another emerging trend regarding whisker reinforced UHTCMCs is laminated  architectures.  Specifically,  set  of  recent  studies  [119,120] have  investigated  the  effect  of  having  porous  UHTC -SiCw  interfaces between  monolithic  UHTC  layers.  Weak  interfaces  such  as  BN  and graphite  have  previously  been  applied  to  induce  crack  deflection  in these lamented structures [121,122]. However, both BN and graphite are prone to oxidation at lower than desirable temperatures ( ≤ 800 ◦ C). Alternatively, a dense SiC layer has the potential to manifest as an undesirably strong interface. A layer composed of a UHTC ceramic with a high  volume  fraction  of  whiskers  could  instead  produce  a  relatively porous  interface  that  can  concomitantly  resist  oxidation  and  induce toughening mechanisms. This was achieved using whiskers of about 18 μ m length and 1.5 μ m diameter. The fabricated architecture is shown in Fig. 5a, where crack deflection and branching are apparent at the SiCw-UHTC  interface.  Fig.  5b  and  c  further  illustrate  how  microscale whisker  pullout  and  bridging  can  enforce  the  macroscale  crack  deflections. The reported flexure strengths were similar to values reported M. Ammendola et al.

Zhang et al. [24] selected pressureless sintering process for producing ZrB2 -SiC composites, which for them nominated SiC volume percentage included 10, 20 and 30%. Sintering occurred at 2000 °C for 3 h. This research revealed that SiC content prevents grain growth, while changing such mechanical properties as /uniFB02 exural strength and toughness to 492 MPa from 404 and to 3.5 MPa m 1/2 from 3, respectively.
Emami et al. [28] produced ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC composites through mechanical activation spark plasma synthesis (MASPSyn) process. For this purpose, selected SiC content was about 31.6 vol% and ZrC content varied from 7.5 to 19.5 vol% and what remained was balanced by ZrB2 as the matrix. The best results for /uniFB02 exural strength and toughness were obtained for 15 vol% of ZrC content by measuring 620 MPa and 5.7 MPa m 1/2 , respectively.
Tsuchida et al. [58] used MA-SHS process to obtain ZrB2 and ZrC from elemental raw materials to produce ZrB2 -ZrC composites through the SPS process. The mechanical characteristics of the composites, estimated by Vickers indentation technique, showed the fracture toughness of 2.9 -5.1 MPa m 1/2 and Vickers hardness of 13.6 -17.8 GPa, depending on the molar ratio of ZrB2/ZrC.

The crystal structure, synthesis, and densification of zirconium diboride (ZrB2) are summarized in detail. In this chapter, ZrB2-ZrC-SiC ceramic was synthesized by reactive hot pressing a mixture of Zr, B4C, and Si powders. The thermal shock resistance of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic was estimated by the water-quenching method and was significantly greater than that of a ZrB2-15vol.% SiC ceramic. The isothermal oxidation of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic was carried out in static air at constant temperatures of 1000±15, 1200±15, and 1400±15 ºC for different amounts of time at each temperature. The mechanism of strength increase for the oxidized specimen indicated that the strength increased with the reaction rate, which was related to the rate of change in volume induced by reaction, initial crack geometry, elastic modulus, and surface free energy. The formation of oxide layers resulted in (I) repair of surface flaws, (II) increase in flexural strength, (III) appearance of a compressive stress zone beneath the surface oxide layers, (IV) decrease in thermal stress, and (V) consumption of thermal stress. These five aspects were favorable to the improvement of the thermal shock resistance of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic. The isothermal oxidation of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic was carried out in static air at 1600±15 ºC. In the different oxidation stages, quantitative models were proposed for predicting oxidation kinetics.

3.1. Microstructural characterization
The continuous and uniform ZrB2-SiC fiber was produced by wet spinning extrusion, with a smooth surface and good toughness. The SEM images of the cross-section of ZrB2-SiC fiber with single and double interfaces  are  shown  in  Fig.  2.  Fig.  2a  and  b  show  the  ZrB2-SiC  fiber diameter  is  about  800 µ m,  and  the  cross-section  is  nearly  circular without large holes, reducing the ZrB2-SiC fiber deformation after sintering. Moreover, the enlarged view of Fig. 2a exhibits that the ZrB2-SiC fiber  with  double  interfaces  has  an  obvious  double-layer  interface structure. The thickness of the SiCw  inner interface layer and ZrB2-SiC outer interface layer is around 10 µ m and 5 µ m respectively, and the thickness ratio is about 2: 1. While that ZrB2-SiC fiber with single interfaces in the enlarged view of Fig. 2b only has a SiCw single-interface layer with a thickness of about 20 µ m. It is worth noting that SiCw with a high aspect ratio produces a bridging phenomenon, which is beneficial to crack deflection, thus consuming more fracture work and improving toughness.

A B S T R A C T
The ZrB2 is widely recognized as an ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) due to its high melting point, high thermal conductivity, and superior  thermal  stability,  making  it  suitable  for  application  at  temperatures ≥ 1400 ◦ C [1 -3]. The ZrB2  exhibits a very remarkable resistance to high-temperature structural degradation, thanks to the in-situ formation of a thermally stable ZrO2-rich oxide scale, further enhancing its overall performance by providing thermal insulation [4 -8]. Furthermore, the addition of SiC as reinforcement in the ZrB2 matrix not only improves its strength and fracture toughness, but also its ability to withstand oxidation and ablation [5,7]. Another advantage of the SiC being present as reinforcement is its ability to impede the growth of ZrB2 grains during the sintering process, resulting in superior densification, leading  to  enhanced  mechanical  properties.  It  has  been  conclusively demonstrated  that  among  the  ZrB2-based  composites  with  different proportions of SiC, the one containing 20 vol % SiC as reinforcement exhibits the most favourable combination of mechanical and thermo-physical properties, along with high resistance to ablation at elevated  temperatures  [9,10].  However,  both  monolithic  ZrB2 and ZrB2 -SiC composites show the same type of brittle catastrophic failure Design  and  optimization  of  the  process  for  fabricating  ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) with continuous carbon fibre (Cf) as a reinforcement has recently garnered significant interest among the researchers globally [12]. The strong interest in this area of research stems from the remarkable improvement in fracture toughness and thermal shock resistance, along with the observation of damage tolerant behaviour in the continuous Cf  reinforced UHTCMCs. Due to the combination of continuous carbon fibre and UHTC matrix, these composites attain the thermal and mechanical properties desirable for  use  in  structural  components  of  hypersonic  vehicles,

3.2. SiC short fiber reinforcement
Other recent investigations have proposed functionally graded materials (FGM) for embedding SiCf  in UHTC matrices. Silvestroni et al. [147] synthesized ZrB2-based composites where the fiber volume fraction  increases  as  a  function  of  the  material  depth,  where  the  thin, outermost layer of the material contains the lowest fiber fraction. In principle, this outermost skin should possess a high resistance against oxidation and ablation, and it serves to protect the inner layers of the FGM  that  are  embedded  with  higher  volume  fractions  of  SiCf,  The described  architecture  merited  a  composite  with  reasonable  fracture toughness values (~6 MPa m ½ ) while maintaining low overall densities; this  suggested  a  potential  for  lightweight  component  design  using fiber-reinforced FGMs. Furthermore, load-displacements curves (Fig.  9b)  indicated  failure  behavior  that  was  not  completely  brittle, based  on  the  tailed  shape  at  higher  displacements.  The  subsequent investigation by Stricker et al. [148] further developed the ZrB2  FGM approach by refining the buffer layer architecture. In this design, the outermost  layer  of  the  composite  contained  a  MoSi2 additive  for enhanced oxidation protection, followed by a ' buffer layer ' that mixed ZrB2 with Si3N4, SiCN, or SiHfBCN. The buffer layer served to obstruct diffusion of Mo species from the outer skin to the inner composite, where it could detrimentally react with SiCf during sintering.
In terms of mechanical properties, SiCf-reinforced composites have reported  advantages  over  monolithic  ceramics,  specifically  in  higher fracture toughness values and retained flexural strength up to 1200 ◦ C. Nevertheless, reaction between the fibers and the matrix phase during sintering  remains  an  ongoing  issue,  which  severely  limits  effective toughening  by  fiber  pullout.  Next  generation  SiC  fibers  with  higher purities  have  the  potential  to  resist  matrix  interaction  during  high temperature pressing, but these materials have not yet been explored as reinforcements for UHTCMCs [149].

3.3. Mechanical properties

While the SiC reinforcement resulted in the improvement in fracture toughness of ZrB2, the average fracture toughness values of all the ZrB2-SiC composites remained in the range of 2-2.7 MPa m 1/2 . Among ZrB2-SiC composites, the composites reinforced with 20% SiC exhibited highest fracture of 2.66 ± 0.34 MPa m 1/2 . For ZrB2-CNT composites, the fracture toughness also increased with increasing CNT reinforcement content and was in the range of 1.5-3.5 MPa m 1/2 . The ZrB2-CNT composites reinforced with 6% CNTs exhibited highest fracture toughness of 3.53 ± 0.47 MPa m 1/2 . Fig. 11 shows the morphologies of indentation cracks propagated through the SPS sintered ZrB2, ZrB2-SiC, and ZrB2-CNT composites. In ZrB2 samples, the crack front was relatively straight without significant bending or deflection (Fig. 11(a)). However, the crack seems to propagate along boundaries of smaller grains (1-2 𝛍 m). The fracture surface of ZrB2 samples indicated predominantly intergranular fracture features with distributed porosity (Fig. 3(a)). For ZrB2-SiC composites, the indentation crack interacts with the reinforced SiC particles causing shear of the finer particles and crack getting deflected at the coarser particles. The fracture surface of the ZrB2-SiC composites also shows predominantly intergranular fracture features with distributed depressions due to grain pull-outs (Fig. 3(b-c)). The relatively higher fracture toughness of these ZrB2-SiC composites compared to ZrB2 samples seems to be due to better densification and additional toughening mechanisms (SiC grain pull-outs and crack deflection).

3.1. Microstructure evolution
The microstructure of the ZrB2 -SiC ceramic matrix and interfaces between the matrix  and  carbon  fibers  were  further  characterized  by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 4a and c shows that the gaps between the micron-sized ZrB2 particles are completely filled with the pyrolytic SiC, and several nanoscale ZrB2 particles are also dispersed within the SiC matrix (indicated by red arrows in Fig. 4c). 3. Results and discussion
Based on a simple rule of mixture calculation, the theoretical density of the ZrB2-SiC-G composites should be 5.07 g/cm 3 . The density of as-prepared ZrB2-SiC-G samples was measured by using the Archimedes method, and the measured bulk densities of the hot-pressed billets ranged from 4.98 g/cm 3 to 5.08 g/cm 3 . All specimens were hot pressed to near theoretical density (98%), with no indication of open porosity. Porosity should not have a significant effect on oxidation behavior based on high relative density and lack of open porosity. Fig. 3 shows the macrographs of the samples after tensile testing at 1550-1750 /C176 C under air atmosphere. The macrographs of the samples appear similar, and the configurational stabilities remained after tensile testing. Fig. 3 shows that a large amount of glass and extensive bubble formed on the surface of the samples after tensile testing at 15501750 /C176 C under air atmosphere. In order to observe the morphology of oxidation products in the outer layer clearly, the cross-section of oxidation samples in the temperature range of 1550-1750 /C176 C were treated with acid solution consisting of HF:HNO3:H2O = 1:1:10 (volume) for 5 s, then the observation performed by means of SEM. The oxide scale thicknesses are shown in Table 1. The thickness of the oxide scale formed at 1750 /C176 C under air atmosphere was 340 l m, which was twice that formed at 1650 /C176 C (160 l m). However, the oxidation behavior of ZrB2-SiC-G composites did not vary significantly at 1550 /C176 C, and the thicknesses of the scale for the samples were only 90 l m. Compared with the thickness of the sample before tensile test, the oxide scale thickness does not change significantly with the increased test temperature. This result suggests that the ZrB2-SiC-G composites have good oxidation resistance under high temperature oxidation and stress.

Figure 8 displays the cross-sectional microstructure of the Cf /ZrB 2 -SiC composite. The sample exhibits a dense cross-sectional morphology. As shown in Figure 8b, micron-scale ZrB 2 are uniformly dispersed within the SiC matrix formed by the reaction between carbon and silicon, and the ZrB 2 -SiC multiphase matrix infiltrates the interior of the fiber bundles, forming a three-dimensional interpenetrating network structure. Furthermore, the in-situ reaction between phenolic resin-derived carbon and free silicon generates SiC, accompanied by volumetric expansion. This expansion compacts the carbon fibers and closes microcracks, thereby enhancing the overall stiffness of the composite. However, the reactive melt infiltration process causes partial erosion to the PyC coating, while the internal fibers remain structurally intact.

1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.

3.3. Correlating interfaces with the mechanical properties
In summary, a correlation of the microstructural features with enhancement in the mechanical properties is established for ZrB2-SiCCNT composites and schematically presented in Fig. 7. A comparison of the mechanical properties of ZrB2-SiC-CNT composites (see Fig. 7 a ), unequivocally, establishes the synergy of SiC and CNT reinforcement in enhancing the properties of ZrB2. The enhancement in the fracture strength, fracture toughness, elastic modulus and reinforcement factor was found to synergistically increase with both SiC and CNT addition (in Z20S10C when compared with monolithic ZrB2). Further, based on the microstructural evolution and enhancement in the fracture toughness, the toughening mechanism with SiC and CNT reinforcement has been delineated, schematically shown in Fig. 7 b . It was found that the strong interfacing between the reinforcement and the matrix, and phenomenon like crack de /uniFB02 ection, branching and bridging (shown in Figs. 3, 5 and 6) attributes in enhancing the strength and toughness of ZrB2-SiC-CNT composites. The result revealed that the enhanced mechanical performance of Z20S10C get an edge over other ZrB2based composites making it a suitable candidate structural material for high damage tolerance.

3. Results and discussion
The bulk density of the ZrB2-20vol.% SiCW composites hotpressed at 2000 ◦ C was 5.44g/cm 3 , which corresponds to 98.6% of the theoretical density estimated with the rule of mixture. The flexural strength and fracture toughness of ZrB2-SiCW composites at room temperature are 510 ± 25MPa and 4.05 ± 0.20MPam 1/2 , respectively. However, the flexural strength and the fracture toughnessofSiCwhisker-reinforcedZrB2 werenotsignificantlyincreased compared with those without reinforcements. The values of flexural strength and the fracture toughness are similar to those of SiC particle reinforced ZrB2 composites. Amazingly, SiC whiskers have been degenerated into particles (Fig. 2). Furthermore, the microstructure of the SiC whisker reinforced ZrB2 composite is very similar to that of the SiC particle reinforced (not shown).

2.3. Phase composition and microstructure characterization
The density and relative density of the Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composite were measured by Archimedes ' method. The open porosity of the Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composite was measured using a MicroActive AutoPore V9600 mercury intrusion  porosimeter  (Micromeritics,  USA).  The  microstructure  and elemental distribution were characterized by a SEM (HELIOS Nanolab 600i, FEI, USA) coupled with an EDS. The multifunctional TEM (Talos F200X, FEI, USA) was used to further characterize the microstructure of the Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composite at nanoscale. The TEM samples were prepared  from  the  Cf/ZrB2 -SiC  composite  through  focused  ion  beam method. The phase composition of the Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composite before and after ablation was identified by an X-ray diffractometer (X ' PERT, PANalytical B.V., Netherlands).

A B S T R A C T
As the total thermal conduction in diborides comprises both electronic and phononic component,   electrical conductivity ( σ e) at room temperature  has  also  been  assessed.  The  electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck coefficient (S) of the HfB2-ZrB2-based composites was measured in the He atmosphere at room temperature using ZEM-3M10 apparatus (ULVAC-RICO Inc.). The figure of  merit  ( ZT = S 2 σ eT κ )  was  calculated, Table  2,  from  thermoelectric  parameters  (electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck  coefficient).  Seebeck  coefficient  is  observed  to  be  negative, suggesting n-type semiconductor behavior, with electrons to be dominant charge carriers in all the composites, and following similar trend of electrical conductivity at room temperature with maximum of -3.57 V/K observed for HZ20S6C. The power factor is ~6 × 10 -5 Wm -1 K 2 at room temperature for Z20S, HZ20S and HZ20S6C composites (Table 2) except for H20S (where the presence of HfB2  phase dominates). On the other hand, ZT value for Z20S is ~43 times to that of H20S. After incorporation of CNT, all thermoelectric parameters in HZ20S6C are observed to increase ~4 -6% as compared to that of HZ20S. Low ZT value in ~1 × 10 -4 Wm -1 K -1 range (due to high κ ) for these composites is not sufficient for thermoelectric applications at room temperature.

The room temperature κ of ZrB2  also decreases by ~40% on introduction of HfB2  in the matrix without much difference in charge carrier density (similar electronic structure, AlB2) but rather, due to difference in phonon scattering and formation of solid solution of (Hf-Zr)B2  [12]. The addition of 10 vol% CNT to ZrB2 + 20 vol% SiC has elicited marginal increase in high temperature (1200 ◦ C) thermal conductivity to ~52.3 Wm -1 K -1 [10].

10. Challenges, emerging trends, and conclusions
UHTCs  including  ZrB2,  HfB2,  TiB2,  TaC,  HfC,  TiC,  etc.  possess admirable  properties;  however,  ZrB2  can  be  considered  as  the  most suitable UHTC material in its monolithic form due to its lower density (6.10  gcm GLYPH<0> 3 ),  chemical  inertness,  high  thermal  conductivity  (56 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 ), high melting temperature and thermal conductivity of its oxide  (~2660 ◦ C  and  ~2  Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 respectively)  proving  strong candidature of ZrB2 for TPS of hypersonic vehicles. It may be highlighted that the starting powder purity and sintered densification play a very significant role in dictating the structural and high temperature oxidation properties, and merely extrapolated prediction of porous composites may not do justice to with that of phase-pure and dense ZrB2-HfB2SiC based composites. Pressure-assisted sintering techniques such as SPS and HP can provide remarkable properties. Among the second phase sintering additives, SiC results in enhancing the densification, mechanical and oxidative performance of ZrB2. On one hand where > 20% SiC content provides an enhanced fracture strength and low temperature oxidation resistance, whereas on the other hand < 20% SiC content assists high temperature performance of ZrB2-based UHTCs. Further, the finer size of SiC ( < 5 μ m) with optimum content (20 vol %) may impart superior combination of thermo-mechanical properties in ZrB2. However,  incorporation  of  HfB2 may  further  limit  the  oxygen  diffusion through the formation of HfO2 and enhance the oxidation resistance of ZrB2 -SiC-based UHTCs. Further, with higher thermal conductivity of HfB2 (~105 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 ), its incorporation has provided ~30% enhanced oxidation  resistance  in

3.1.1. Theoretical calculation of diffusion coefficient
The average diffusion coefficient for Hf in ZrB2  was observed to be higher  (i.e.,  ~10.2 × 10 GLYPH<0> 13 m 2 /s)  than  that  of  Zr  into  HfB2 (i.e., ~2.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 13 m 2 /s) which is also supported by higher diffusion depth of Hf into ZrB2. Diffusion coefficient calculated in the present work is in accordance with the lattice diffusion of hot pressed ZrB2 at 2100 ◦ C [44], but diffusion coefficient alone cannot justify the mechanism. Hassan et. al. [35] reported the grain boundary diffusion to be dominating mechanism at temperatures < 1950 ◦ C for pure ZrB2  phase, however, with 20 vol%  SiC  addition,  lattice  diffusion  dominates  at  temperatures > 1850 ◦ C, which is very close to the joining temperature used in the present work. Furthermore, recent study by Dubey et. al. [45] also reported the transition from grain boundary to lattice diffusion at 1850 ◦ C for pure HfB2, which might shift to lower values with SiC reinforcement. Therefore,  during  joining  of  ZS-HS  composite  via  SPS  at  1800 ◦ C, 40 MPa pressure, the diffusion mechanism is dominated by the grain boundary diffusion along with some transition to the lattice diffusion as suggested by the calculated diffusion coefficient value (i.e., 2.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 9 m 2 /s).

4. Discussion
Thermal transport in solid materials is a consequence of electron and phonon conduction. Therefore, thermal conductivity is explained in terms of an electronic contribution and a phonon contribution. The phonon contribution depends on number of carriers, phonon velocity and phonon mean free path, which are all constant at temperatures above the Debye temperature. Debye temperatures for ZrB2, HfB2, SiC and La2O3 are 750 K, 30 565 K, 2 1080 K 31 and 160 K, 28 respectively. Therefore, the phonon contribution to conductivity should be constant at temperatures well above the Debye ( ∼ 1000 ◦ C) temperature as has been highlighted for pure ZrB2 and HfB2. 27 Grain boundaries are resistive elements for thermal transport leading to so-called Kapitza resistance. Therefore, thermal conductivity decreases with reduced grain size. 32 The intrinsic Kapitza resistance should be relatively temperature insensitive above the Debye temperature unless physical changes take place with increasing temperature. 18 The dominant part of thermal conductivity of monolithic ZrB2 and HfB2 is the electron contribution which could represent up to 90% of the thermal conductivity in ZrB2 and HfB2 ceramics. 17 SiC addition is known to increase the phonon contribution to thermal conductivity. 17 However, data on the effect of SiC on UHTC thermal conductivity are contradictory. Due to its high thermal conductivity 33 compared with ZrB2 and HfB2, SiC should increase thermal conductivity and the greater the amount of SiC added, the higher the thermal conductivity as a better connected internal network is formed. 13,14,18,34 However, the presence of SiC-ZrB2 or SiC-HfB2 in addition to ZrB2-ZrB2 or HfB2-HfB2 interfaces can lower thermal conductivity below monolithic thermal conductivities values 15,27 as a consequence of Kapitza resistance.

4. Discussion

At high C concentrations, interconnected C-containing secondary phases could form, providing an easy heat flow path while at low concentrations there is no interconnected network and Kapitza resistances (SiC-ZrB2 or C-ZrB2, SiC-HfB2 or C-HfB2 interfaces) become dominant lowering thermal conductivity compared to the monolith. SiC hard agglomerates found in HfB2-based UHTCs 22 lower thermal conductivity compared to HfB2 as there is no interconnected network as occurs in the ZrB2-based UHTCs with well-dispersed SiC. In addition, internal stresses in SiC-ZrB2 or SiC-HfB2 and fracture toughness were lower in HfB2-based UHTCs than in ZrB2-based UHTCs, 22 which may arise due to the presence of partial debonding between adjacent SiC and HfB2 grains. Microcracking was not observed during microstructural characterization. 22 Microcracking is known to degrade thermal transport in ceramics 36,37 and debonding produces the same effect as Kapitza resistance increase. In conclusion, ZrB2-based UHTCs have a lower reduction of thermal conductivity than HfB2-based UHTCs compared to monolithic UHTCs, as a consequence of the SiC being agglomerate free and homogeneously dispersed. Therefore, dispersion of secondary phase throughout the composite volume plays a leading role in thermal transport efficiency.

3. Results and discussion
Fig. 4 shows the cross-sectional images of FE-SEM and EDS analysis corresponding each process condition from Cases A to G. Cases A and B show the specimens coated with ZrC-49 powder, which presents the severely damaged surfaces due to the impact of high velocity un-melted large particles during the coating process. In Case A, as seen in Fig. 4(a), the underlying C/C composite was exposed and damaged. The reduced damages in Case B [Fig. 4(b)] can be attributed to the increased power and addition of He gas, resulting in decrease of un-melted powder in the plasma flame. However, since the plasma condition might not be sufficient to melt the whole injected particles, the surface damages were still observed  and  the  thin  ZrC  layer  was  partly  coated.  To  mitigate  the damages and to increase coating thickness, a relatively small powder (ZrC-42) was introduced with different current and pressure conditions as described as Cases C, D and E in Table 1. In Case C, it was confirmed that  the  SiC  layer  was  partially  damaged,  while  the  underlying  C/C composite was found to be relatively intact. However, as seen in Fig. 4 (c),  it  was  hard  to  increase  the  ZrC  layer  coating  thickness  only  by reducing the powder size under the same processing conditions. To increase the contact time of particles with the plasma, the gas flow rate was reduced but the chamber pressure was increased for Cases D and E. Relatively thick ZrC layers of 12.5 μ m (Case D) and 25 μ m (Case E) were obtained, which indicated that not only the smaller size of powder but also their sufficient residence time in the plasma were crucial to ensure complete  melting  and  to  form  a  thick  coating  layer.  Therefore,  the coating processes under high power (54 kW) and high pressure (150 mbar) conditions was performed with smaller-sized powders, ZrC-33 (Case  F)  and  ZrC-10  (Case  G). In this work, therefore, we propose a multi-step ZrC coating process on C/C composites to form a homogeneous ZrC multi-layer protecting the  base  material  under  high-temperature  oxidation  conditions.  The structural properties of the ZrC layers formed through multi-step coatings  were  investigated  using  field  emission  scanning  electron  microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), a Vickers hardness tester, and a  surface  roughness tester.  The  performance  of  the  ZrC  layers  under high-temperature  oxidation  conditions  was  evaluated  using  a  highvelocity  oxygen  fuel  (HVOF)  facility.  The  results  were  compared  to those of layers obtained by a conventional single-step coating process.

3. Results and discussion
